You must include at least one sassy potted succulent that judges everyone who walks past it, one leftover slice of vanilla birthday cake that’s been in the fridge for 3 weeks and is very proud of its crisp frosting edges, and one pair of fluffy oven mitts that are extremely smug about being the only kitchen item that gets to hug hot things. Make them as silly, meandering, and unapologetically pointless as you want—no need for them to be "good" or "useful" in any way, just have fun with it.

dream_response:
### 1. Mabel the Sassy Succulent
Mabel the fuzzy echeveria has lived on the mismatched pine windowsill of Joe’s Corner Coffee for 17 months, and she has Opinions. When the barista drops a large oat milk latte and curses loud enough to make the salt shaker rattle, Mabel rolls her plump, pale green leaves into a perfect, unimpressed half-moon. The guy in the vintage Patagonia vest who stops to take a selfie with her, knocking her terracotta pot an inch to the left? She blushes a faint, judgmental pink at her leaf tips, the kind that says “I have seen 10,000 people take worse photos of smaller plants and you are not special.” The toddler who drops a half-sucked cherry lollipop stick next to her pot gets a pass, mostly because Mabel can smell the faint artificial cherry residue on the wood, and she’s decided it counts as a very small, very weird tribute. When the lunch rush hits and 12 people walk past without looking where they’re going, Mabel spends the whole 20 minutes silently roasting their shoe choices, their terrible “live, laugh, love” tote bags, and the guy who tried to pay for a latte with a jar of loose change. No one ever waters her too much, no one ever knocks her over completely, and she gets the best indirect sunlight in the whole shop. She’s the most successful being in the building, and she will not be taking feedback.

---

### 2. Chip the Birthday Cake Slice
Chip has been in the back of the top shelf of the 3rd floor dorm fridge for 22 days, and he has never been prouder. He was cut from a round vanilla bean cake at Mia’s 20th birthday party, frosted with vanilla buttercream and dotted with rainbow sprinkles, and he still has the little crumb chip on his bottom left corner from when Mia’s roommate cut him a little too aggressively. The best part of his life right now is his edges: the cold fridge air has turned his top and side frosting into a crisp, sugary shell that crunches just a little if you tap it with a fingernail, the kind of perfect, un-soggy edge that 99% of birthday cake slices never get to have. The block of sharp cheddar next to him has been complaining about his own mold spots for three days, and Chip ignores him completely. When the fridge light flicks on at 2pm when a sophomore comes in for a gatorade, Chip gets to show off his crisp edges to the half-eaten bagel in the door shelf, who nods in awe. The sophomore grabs the questionable leftover burrito in the middle shelf and doesn’t even glance at Chip, and Chip feels a surge of triumph: he’s still here, his edges are still crisp, no one is going to eat him, and he gets to listen to the cheddar complain every time the fridge opens. Last week a tiny ice crystal formed on his top sprinkle, and he’s been wearing it like a tiny, fancy tiara ever since.

---

### 3. Fluff and Puff the Oven Mitts
Fluff and Puff are a pair of quilted bright yellow cotton oven mitts, hanging on the hook right next to the stove in Lila’s studio apartment, and they are the only kitchen items that get to hug hot things, and they will never let anyone forget it. When Lila makes chocolate chip cookies on Saturday, Fluff gets pulled off the hook first to grab the hot cookie sheet, and he spends the whole 12 minutes the cookies bake gloating at the wooden spoon, who only ever gets to stir cold batter. Puff gets to grab the cast iron skillet of roasted sweet potatoes 10 minutes later, and he’s so giddy he flops a little against Lila’s wrist when she pulls him off the hook. They judge the spatula for only ever scraping bowls of cold frosting, the dish towel for only wiping up spills, the tiny tea kettle for only ever holding lukewarm water. When Lila accidentally brushes her finger against the hot oven rack and yelps, Fluff and Puff exchange a smug, silent look over the top of the stove: only they get to touch 400-degree surfaces without getting burned, only they get the honor of hugging fresh-baked cookies and crispy roasted veggies. At the end of the night, Lila hangs them back up on the hook, still warm from the oven, and they spend the next hour bragging to each other about how Fluff got to hold a cookie sheet with 18 perfect cookies on it, and Puff got to carry a skillet full of caramelized sweet potato edges. The spatula rolls its silicone head at them, and they don’t even care.

dream_response:
My alarm goes off when the Seattle mist beads up on the chipped rim of my mug home, each droplet cool enough to tickle the moss fuzz on my cheeks when I lean over the edge to catch them. I trundle up the curved, tea-stained interior of the mug, my tiny clawed feet catching on the faint brown ring where the human used to steep chamomile every night before they abandoned me here three weeks ago—every dewdrop that forms overnight tastes like that honeyed chamomile, faint and warm, with a little hint of the raw honey the human stirred in last Tuesday. I sip three in a row, one of them catching a dandelion seed that drifts in with the mist, which makes me sneeze so hard I almost topple off the rim, and I have to catch myself on the chip that serves as my front door. After that, I fluff the moss blanket I keep tucked in the bottom of the mug, knock a stray crumb of lemon scone that rolled in last night into my stash pocket, and tuck a fresh dewdrop into a hollow in my sea glass collection to keep it sweet for my afternoon snack.

By the time Marigold the orb weaver shows up on the railing, I’m already perched on the chip with my bottle cap fragments laid out in a neat row, ready to trade. She shakes her front leg to drop the snippet of radio static she caught in her web overnight—this time it’s the tail end of a 1960s folk song, the singer’s voice crackling like pop rocks in my ears, and I hand her three shiny red caps that look like tiny rubies in the drizzle light. We sit there for a bit while she mends a broken strand of her web, me munching on the scone crumb, until I glance over at the new plump rosette succulent the human plopped on the fire escape two days ago, sitting smack in the middle of my favorite 10 a.m. sunbeam. I huff and flick a tiny piece of moss at its thick leaves, grumbling that it’s hogging all the warmth and I had to curl up in the mug’s shadow for an extra hour yesterday waiting for the beam to shift. I threaten to drop a whole dewdrop on its head later if it doesn’t budge, but I know it won’t, so I just sigh and shift to the cooler, shadier part of the rim to finish my crumb, while Marigold chitters what sounds like very firm agreement.

dream_response:
The weekly Sidewalk Crack Neighborhood potluck kicks off right as the afternoon sun hits the 2mm gap at a 45-degree angle, the fuzzy black sock (lost outside a suburban soccer field in October 2021, still crusted with faint orange Cheeto dust from its last wear) uncurling first to greet the brass paperclip that trundles over still snagged to a frayed corner of a 2018 college thesis draft page it’s been hauling around for weeks. The iridescent blue sea glass (dropped by a toddler in a sparkly tutu on a rainy 2020 sidewalk, still smudged with faint strawberry jam) bounces in next, setting down a carefully collected pile of dewdrops it caught overnight in a curled dandelion leaf, while the single yellow Lego brick trundles over on its two studs, dragging a tiny scrap of blue sidewalk chalk it found snagged in a crack last week, and the 2019 grandma’s thimble tinks in last, balancing a minuscule thimbleful of lemonade it siphoned from a drippy iced tea glass left on a porch rail that morning. Inside jokes fly fast: the paperclip gets roasted for the three days it spent stuck to a dandelion seed last spring when it tried to climb the concrete wall to “see the giant sky,” the Lego brick gets teased for its never-ending failed quest to stack itself on the sea glass (it slides off every single time, no matter how carefully it positions its studs), and everyone groans when the sock brings up the time it accidentally got stuck to a kid’s backpack and was dragged three blocks before shaking loose in a parking lot.

They pass the dishes around first, everyone oohing over the sock’s Cheeto dust (the most popular potluck item by a landslide, salty and perfect for dipping tiny crumbs of chalk into) and the thimble’s lemonade, tart enough to make the sea glass glow a little brighter with delight. The paperclip unfolds its thesis page scrap to show off the new doodle a college student drew of a cat in the margin when they were procrastinating writing, the Lego brick finally manages to balance on the sea glass for three full seconds to thunderous (if very quiet) cheers from the group, and the sock tells a terrible joke about why sidewalk cracks never get lonely that makes everyone snort so hard a nearby ant drops its crumb of sugar. When the sun dips low enough that the concrete starts to cool, they tuck their leftover snacks into tiny nooks in the crack, the sock curls up around the Lego brick to keep it warm overnight, the paperclip leans against the sea glass, and the thimble tucks itself into a crevice lined with a fallen dandelion seed, all of them content to nap until next week’s potluck, no plans, no chores, no need to be anything other than the small, lost, perfectly happy things they are.

dream_request: Write a short story about a dragon who is terrified of heights, and who becomes a successful pastry chef in a bustling city.
dream_prompt: Write a short story about a dragon who is terrified of heights, and who becomes a successful pastry chef in a bustling city.
dream_response:
Crumb the dragon never intended to become a pastry chef. He’d only crash-landed in the city’s bakery district after a particularly ill-advised attempt to fly off the town clock tower (he’d heard the pigeons bragging about the view). The 40-foot tumble left him with a permanent phobia of anything higher than the top of his kitchen counter, and a lingering aversion to wind that smells like chimney smoke.

He’d stumbled into an abandoned ground-floor storefront to wait out the rain that day, found a half-bag of flour and a jar of lavender honey in the back, and tried to make a snack. A stray wisp of fire—too strong for the tiny camp stove he’d found, too uncontrolled for the old brick oven (which was mounted way too high on the wall for his comfort, anyway)—accidentally caramelized the honey glaze on his dough perfectly. He took a bite, and his tail flicked so hard it knocked over a stack of mixing bowls.

He never left.

He filed his horns down so he wouldn’t bump the low ceiling, sewed himself a frilly white apron dotted with embroidered croissants, and taught himself to channel his fire breath into precise, papery wisp that melted sugar and honey without burning pastry. He didn’t need the oven at all. His honey-lavender croissants, glazed with a single controlled burst of fire, became the most sought-after treat in the district. The construction workers who ate lunch on the stoop outside loved his buttery meat pies. The neighborhood kids begged for his dragon-shaped shortbread, which he iced with little smiley faces using a claw-tipped piping bag. He never climbed above the first step of his tiny wooden step stool, which he used only to reach the jar of rainbow sprinkles on the highest shelf. He fed leftover cookie crumbs to the pigeons that perched on his windowsill, and left extra buttery crusts out the back door for the foxes that lived in the alley.

No one was scared of Crumb once they tried his food. He wore bright red oven mitts with croissant patterns, kept a dandelion Lila, his 7-year-old regular customer, gave him every Tuesday tucked in his apron pocket, and only ever breathed fire when he was glazing pastries or toasting the marshmallows he put in hot chocolate for shivering kids in the winter.

When the city announced its annual Grand Baking Competition, with a prize ticket to the Cloud Peak Pastry Festival perched at the summit of the 12,000-foot mountain that loomed over the town, the other local bakers nominated Crumb without telling him first. He panicked when he found out—he couldn’t go up a mountain, that was way higher than his counter!

But Lila and the regulars had already handled it. They’d called the festival organizers, who had a permanent ground-level vendor pavilion for attendees with mobility or height-related fears. They’d arranged for a low-slung, wide carriage with soft wool blankets and no high seats to take him up the winding, slow mountain road. No flying, no high viewpoints, just a gentle ride that stopped every hour so he could stretch his wings and eat a honey croissant.

At the competition, the snooty head judge Madame Gâteau raised an eyebrow when Crumb waddled in, oven mitts tucked under his claws, but he set up his station on a low table the organizers provided: his batch of croissant dough, his jar of local lavender honey, his tiny piping bag of vanilla buttercream. When it was time to finish his entry—a 3-foot tall croissant tower layered with honey cream and dotted with his dragon-shaped shortbread—he breathed the tiniest, most controlled wisp of fire, just enough to melt the sugar glaze on top without burning the delicate, flaky layers. The crowd gasped. No one had ever caramelized a glaze without an oven before. The croissants were warm, the lavender was perfectly balanced, the honey sweet but not cloying. Madame Gâteau took a bite, and the corner of her judging ribbon got a tiny singe when Crumb whooped so loud he accidentally let out a second, tiny fire puff. She laughed, and said the singe was the best part of the prize.

He won first place. The prize included the trip to Cloud Peak, a year’s supply of organic lavender, and a permanent stall at the festival’s ground-level pavilion for as long as he wanted to participate. The city even renamed the alleyway his bakery sat on Crumb Lane, and put up a little sign with a cartoon dragon holding a croissant on it.

Crumb still hasn’t gotten over his fear of heights. He still won’t climb more than one step on his stool, still refuses to go anywhere that makes him feel like he’s “floating” more than an inch off the ground, still makes Lila sit on the floor if she wants a hug at his bakery door. But he doesn’t need to go high to be happy. All the best things—warm croissants fresh off his fire glaze, dandelions from Lila, foxes waiting for crusts by the back door, pigeons perched on his windowsill—are all right at ground level, where he’s safe, and where he can share the thing he loves most with everyone who stops by.

dream_response:
Ugh, sparrows are the absolute worst. Half-eaten blackberry pulp all matted in my moss right now, it’s gonna be sticky for at least two hours, I can feel it clumping the clover threads on my left cheek. I flicked a tiny sphagnum splinter at the little guy but he just did that dumb chirp where he bobs his head like he’s proud of himself, little striped menace.
My dewdrop tea’s still steeping, I hope. I left it on the twig shelf when I went to check the button stash near the blue bench, the one where the teen girls sit and share blue raspberry cotton candy and drop all their button tops when they’re laughing too hard to notice they’re falling off their jackets. Found a sparkly iridescent one this morning, purple, has a little star etched on it, perfect for trading the squirrel crew for acorn caps. Last trade I got three, one’s my tea sieve, one’s my rain blanket, one I’m trying to mold to fit Nigel’s shell, he keeps outgrowing it, snails grow so fast when it rains, I don’t get how that works, he’s only been around three months and he’s already twice the size he was when I found him stuck in a dewdrop on a daisy last May.
Nigel’s currently sulking in the crook near the thyme patch, I made him a moss doll yesterday with shiny plastic sequin eyes and a dandelion stem sash, he knocked it into my tea last night when he was trying to climb the shelf to get at a dewdrop that looked bigger than the others. Soggy mess now, smells like dandelion fluff and pine resin, he won’t even look at me, just keeps slowly sliding his shell back and forth across the moss like he’s mad.
Remember that raccoon I tried to teach leaf origami to last week? What a disaster. Spent 45 minutes folding a maple leaf into a tiny boat for him, showing him each crease, and he just kept trying to shove the whole thing in his mouth. Then he stole my tea cup, which is a periwinkle snail shell I found last summer, ran off across the grass like a tiny striped bandit. I chased him, tripped over a dandelion root, got a whole clover sprout stuck in my side for three days, couldn’t get it out without pulling out a chunk of sphagnum. He came back the next day and left a crinkled blue candy wrapper at the base of the tree as an apology, I use it as a tablecloth for when I have snail tea parties. The time before that I tried to give him a tiny moss cookie I made, he ate it and then tried to eat my hat, I had to hide in the bark hollow for an hour.
Patricia the centipede who lives in the bark next to my spot tapped on my wall this morning with a tiny pebble, said the aphids are being extra annoying this year, dripping honeydew all over the north side of the tree so it’s all sticky. I told her I’d leave some of the leftover berry pulp I was saving for Nigel for her, she loves the sweet sticky stuff, it makes her legs extra shiny, she showed me last week, I was very impressed. She left me a tiny piece of quartz she found under a rock yesterday, I’m using it as a tea coaster now.
Sometimes the little kids come by and leave trinkits, you know? A marble with a swirl in it last week, a sequin shaped like a fish, a tiny plastic T-Rex that’s missing its left arm. I keep them all tucked between the bark and the moss, I like to arrange them in little scenes when it’s raining too hard to go forage for dewdrops. The worst is the park guys with the leaf blowers, one day a year, I have to hide in the hollow at the bottom of the trunk, wrap myself around a root so I don’t get blown into the next county. Lost my favorite acorn cap hat that way two years ago, still miss it, it had a little acorn nut still stuck in the top and a ladybug painted on the side with berry juice.
I tried to grow a tiny clover garden in my crook last spring, but the squirrels kept digging it up to bury acorns. One of them even dug up a clover I’d named Clara, I was very upset. Now it’s just dandelions and that one patch of creeping thyme that smells like lemon when it rains, that’s nice. I rub a little thyme on my moss when I’m cold, it warms me up for a bit, like a tiny moss heating pad.
Oh! Right, I almost forgot, yesterday I found a tiny silver thimble near the bench, the one the old lady sits on who feeds the pigeons pieces of bread crust. I think I’m gonna save that for the next squirrel trade, they love shiny metal things, maybe I can get five acorn caps this time, I could make a tiny canopy for Nigel’s home so he doesn’t get rained on when he’s out climbing the oak’s lower branches.
The sparrow’s back, he’s got a friend, they’re eyeing my tea. If they drop any more pulp I’m gonna launch a whole moss clod at them, I don’t care if they tell their whole sparrow crew and no one leaves me old bread crumbs anymore. I’ve got enough sticky moss for one day, thank you very much.

dream_response:
### Friday 3:12pm – Monday 3:12pm (72 hours, on the dot)
The email hits at 3:12pm on a crisp Vermont October Friday, when Elara Voss (37, retired Vermont State Archive conservator, 18 months into running Lichen Bound, a 102 sq ft storefront that exclusively sells out-of-print North American lichen field guides, no exceptions) is trying to re-glue the spine of a 1967 *Field Guide to the Lichens of the Great Lakes Dune Systems*. Her chonky 16lb orange tabby Marmalade (named for his thallus-shaped white patch on his left front paw) is napping on a stack of 1970s *Lichen Flora of the Pacific Northwest* paperbacks she uses as a footstool, snoring loud enough to rattle the cross-stitch above the register that reads *LICHENS ARE ORIGINAL SLOW FOOD*.
The email is from Dr. Elspeth Hale, 98, archivist for the reclusive 100-year-old Mycological Society of the Green Mountains (website last updated 1998, hosted on Geocities, font looks like it was typed on a 1947 Underwood typewriter). Subject line: RE: Unexpected Lichen Submissions from Feline Contributor. The body offers a $5000 unrestricted grant if Elara can produce a 12-page zine of Marmalade’s “specimen contributions” within three weeks, noting the society has a long (if little-documented) history of accepting cat-delivered lichens: the last was a calico in rural Maine who brought them a new *Parmelia* species in 1987.
Elara drops the 1967 field guide. The spine cracks. Marmalade opens one green eye, licks a paw, and goes back to sleep.

#### Friday breakfast (eaten at the store counter, 8:17am, before the email):
Steel-cut oats simmered for 45 minutes with local maple syrup, a dash of Ceylon cinnamon, and a side of pickled radishes from the Saturday farmer’s market that she jarred two weeks prior. Black coffee from a chipped mug printed with *I <3 Soredia* (an inside joke from her archive days, referencing asexual lichen spores). A single bite of leftover apple cider donut from the bakery down the street, which she dropped half of on the floor, so Marmalade got the other half.

By Friday 5:30pm, Elara has pulled the tattered 1952 *Lichens of the Adirondacks* she’s been using as a scratchpad for the last six months. Tucked between the pages are 37 of Marmalade’s “gifts”: dried specimens, each labeled with date and rough location (she started tracking after he dropped a neon-orange *Xanthoria polycarpa* on her laptop three months prior, which she couldn’t ID for three days until a grad student from the local college wandered in). Two are previously unrecorded in Vermont: a *Cladonia squamosa* var. *squamulosa* not documented in the state since 1921, and a tiny crustose *Lecanora strobilina* she found stuck to his whiskers last week. She spends the next three hours typing up specimen notes, photographing each with her 12-year-old Canon point-and-shoot, the flash occasionally reflecting off the store’s single 60-watt bulb.
Dinner is a grilled cheese with extra-sharp cheddar and thin-sliced Honeycrisp apple, eaten while scrolling the society’s Geocities site. She finds a 1993 newsletter scanned to the site: the 1987 Maine calico was named *Lichen* (obviously), and her owner was a retired lighthouse keeper who got a $3000 grant (adjusted for inflation, roughly the same as the current offer) to produce a 10-page zine.

#### Saturday breakfast (eaten standing at the kitchen sink of her apartment above the store, 9:42am):
Buckwheat pancakes drizzled with blueberry maple syrup, a side of roasted Brussels sprouts she meal-prepped the night prior, and dandelion root tea sweetened with a spoonful of honey. Marmalade sits on the counter batting at a fruit fly, stealing a tiny corner of pancake (no syrup) when she’s not looking.

The first customer of the day is a 16-year-old parkour kid in a mushroom hoodie, who bangs the door open hard enough to make the bell above it jingle so loud Marmalade bolts under the register. He asks for three field guides, verbatim:
1. *Field Guide to the Lichens of Old Growth Redwood Forests* (1979, Henry Imshaug, cover photo of *Ramalina menziesii* draped over a redwood branch, spine torn at the top)
2. *Microlichens of the Northeastern United States: A Photographic Key* (1992, William and Carol Culberson, spiral bound, coffee stain on page 42)
3. *Lichens and Indigenous Material Culture of the Pacific Northwest* (1984, edited by Haida ethnobotanist Florence Davidson, the only copy in the store, has a handwritten note from the previous owner: “Found this in a dumpster behind UW Anthro building, 1990. Do not sell to tourists.”)
Elara lets him hold the Davidson book but refuses to sell it, tells him to come back in 10 years when she’s ready to part with it. He leaves a folded $5 bill on the counter for a sticker anyway, and Elara tucks the bill into the charity jar for the local food bank.
At 2:17pm, Elara heads to the post office to mail preliminary dried specimens (archival sleeved, with GPS notes) to Dr. Hale. The package is 1/8 inch too thick to count as a letter. The postal worker on duty is Dale, a guy she’s known since she opened the store, who works every Saturday.
“This is a parcel, Elara,” Dale says, tapping the edge of the mailer with a pen. “Parcel rate is $11.50. You can’t just bend the rules because your cat is a ‘specimen collector’.”
“It’s flexible, see?” Elara pushes on the cardboard, and it creases permanently down the middle. “You just have to push hard enough.”
“That’s not USPS flexible. Flexible means it bounces back. That’s just creased.”
They squabble for five minutes, Elara calling him a “lichen-hating bureaucrat”, Dale calling her “a crazy cat lady trying to cheat the post office out of 90 cents”. She ends up paying the $11.50, and Dale slips her a sheet of morel-printed “forever” stamps. “Dr. Hale already called to confirm delivery of your last package,” he says, smirking. “Said the calico in Maine never brought her anything this exciting. Tell your cat he’s got a fan club.”

Dinner Saturday is pesto pasta with basil grown on her windowsill, topped with the last of the parmesan from her fridge. Marmalade steals a piece of chicken off her plate while she’s in the bathroom, and she spends 20 minutes shooing him out from under the register where he’s hiding before she can finish typing the zine’s table of contents.

#### Sunday breakfast (eaten at the store counter, 10:03am):
Sourdough toast slathered with tahini and sliced strawberries, a mug of orange juice pressed from oranges she bought at the market the week prior. Marmalade sits on the counter staring at her the entire time, and she gives him the heel of the toast with no tahini.

She spends Sunday afternoon following Marmalade into the woods behind her house. He trots ahead of her, tail up, until he reaches a stand of 80-year-old paper birches, where he stops and waits for her. At the base of the largest birch is a small, dry hollow lined with moss, stuffed with half a dozen lichen specimens he never brought her. She takes GPS coordinates, photographs the spot, and adds a blurry photo of the hollow to the zine’s third page.
The power goes out at 6:42pm for 45 minutes. She lights a beeswax candle, sits on the floor of the store with Marmalade curled in her lap, and reads aloud from a 1961 paper on *Cladonia* taxonomy until he falls asleep. When the power comes back on, the register’s cash drawer pops open, and she finds a 1955 *Lichen Flora of the Canadian Rockies* tucked behind it that she forgot she owned: it has a pressed *Cladonia pyxidata* pressed between pages 112 and 113, labeled in pencil “Found on a rock near Lake Louise, 1957. J.M.” She tucks it into her personal collection, not for sale.
Dinner Sunday is leftover pesto pasta, eaten while she formats the zine’s photos. She spills a drop of coffee on the draft’s fourth page, and draws a tiny *Xanthoria* in the stain with a Sharpie.

#### Sunday 11:47pm – The dream:
She’s driving a 1967 Ford Fairlane (the same year as the cracked field guide on her desk) through a junkyard full of rusted 1950s car hoods. Every hood is covered in thick, glowing turquoise caribou lichen (*Cladonia rangiferina*), which pulses like it’s breathing. She gets out of the car, runs a finger over a hood, and the lichen peels off in a soft sheet, revealing a tiny engraved paw print that is exactly the shape of Marmalade’s thallus patch. A bumper sticker on the hood she’s touching reads *I <3 LICHENS*. She wakes up at 3:17am, mouth dry, Marmalade curled on her chest purring, with a fragment of *Lecanora muralis* stuck to his ear, which he must have picked up on his way to bed that night.

#### Monday breakfast (eaten at the store counter, 7:28am):
Miso soup with silken tofu and scallions, a rice ball stuffed with pickled plum, and a matcha latte sweetened with maple syrup. Marmalade sits on the counter staring at her soup, and she gives him a tiny piece of the rice ball, no miso.
A tourist from Ohio wanders in at 11am, asks for a field guide to mosses. Elara tells him the plant nursery down the street carries them, and he leaves a jar of blackberry jam on the counter as a thank you. She eats a spoonful of it while she’s testing paper stocks for the zine: she’s printing the final version on recycled seed paper, so readers can plant the zine after they read it.
At 2:58pm, she’s waiting for Marmalade to come home with one last specimen (he’s been gone for 45 minutes, which is unusual for him). She’s holding the final page of the zine, which has a blank margin at the bottom, when a half-remembered line floats into her head, one she’s halfway sure she read in a 1970s grade school science primer called *The Secret Life of Lichens* that she checked out of the archive once, but can’t find any record of anywhere else.
She scribbles it in blue ballpoint pen right next to a smudge of lichen ink on her finger: *What the woods tuck in his fur, he tucks in the book / no map needed, no lie, no look.*
Marmalade walks in the door at 3:12pm exactly, drops a perfect *Peltigera canina* (dog lichen) at her feet, and headbutts her ankle. She adds the specimen to the zine, hits send on the final PDF to Dr. Hale, and gives Marmalade the rest of the blackberry jam as a reward.

dream_response:
okay so first off i’m mabel, i’m a 1998 paperback copy of the little bear who loved honey, i live on the 3rd shelf of the picture book nook at the mapleton public library, right between gertie (the beat-up 1987 goodnight moon who’s been here longer than any of the librarians) and diesel (the board book about trucks that won’t shut up about how many kids take him to birthday parties). i’ve got three main things that make me me: first, the coffee stain on page 42, that’s from 2019 when mrs. henderson spilled her vanilla latte on me during summer reading program kickoff and never quite got the ring out, it’s right where little bear finds the beehive, i think it makes the page look extra cozy, like the honey spilled a little too. second, my spine cracks every time some kid yanks me open too fast to see the bear pictures, i’ve got three distinct cracks down the middle now, i like to call them my laugh lines. third, the neon pink cat sticker on my back cover, the one with the cowboy hat? some 7-year-old named lila stuck that on me in 2021, i told the librarians i’d scream if they tried to peel it off, so it’s permanent now, i consider it my good luck charm.

we have a secret underground book club, by the way. we meet after closing, behind the return slot, when the night librarian mrs. cho pushes the book return bin aside to get to the old VHS tapes she keeps back there. gertie comes, diesel comes, annie (the tattered teen section copy of anne of green gables who’s obsessed with all the middle schoolers who write hearts in her margins) comes, moo (the farm animal board book that’s missing half her pages, we think a toddler ate one of the cow pages) comes, even that weird beat-up copy of captain underpants that lives in the bathroom display (long story, a kid left him there in 2020 and the librarians never moved him) shows up sometimes, he only gossips about the weird things kids do in the bathroom so we don’t invite him that often.

last night’s meeting was mostly me rambling about the kid who checked me out last week, javi, 5 years old, peanut butter sandwich crumbs all over his jeans. he sat on the library steps reading the part where little bear shares honey with his squirrel friends, and he started crying, because his best friend moved to ohio last month, and he said, quiet-like, “i wish i could share honey with him too” and then he wiped his eyes on page 42, right next to the coffee stain, so now there’s a little salt stain mixed in with the latte ring. gertie grumbled about me getting “emotionally contaminated” but i could tell she was soft for it, she tucked a little piece of orange construction paper into her pages later, said it was “to absorb the sadness” but i know she just wanted to be nice.

diesel kept bragging about being taken to a birthday party last weekend, where a kid used him as a plate for cupcakes, got chocolate all over his truck pictures. annie rolled her eyes so hard i thought her pages would fall out, she was talking about the 12-year-old who checked her out last week and highlighted every single scene where anne and gilbert look at each other, and wrote “MY TRUE LOVE <3” in the margin next to the proposal scene. moo just kept passing around cheerios crumbs she found in a kid’s backpack, she’s the best.

oh! and remember my spine cracks? last week a toddler named leo yanked me open so hard i cracked *again*, right down the middle, and his mom gasped like i was a real person, said “oh no, we broke the book!” and leo just giggled and said “no, he’s laughing!” and i swear i almost floated off the shelf i was so happy. i told the club that the new crack is my favorite part now, it’s proof a kid loved me enough to be rough with me, not just gently turn my pages. gertie said that was “disgustingly sentimental” but i saw her pat her own coffee-stained page later, like she was thinking about the time a kid fell asleep on her and drooled on the goodnight moon page.

also! the cat sticker! last week a little girl named zoe was checking me out, she pointed at the cowboy hat cat and said “cool cat!” and she drew a little lasso around the hat with her sparkly pen, so now it’s got a lasso too. i’m never getting that sticker off, even if the librarians try. i’m thinking of asking lila, if she ever comes back to the library, to draw a little saddle on the cat too.

oh right, the other day the librarians put me in the halloween display, next to all the spooky books about ghosts and werewolves, and i hated it. the ghost book kept telling scary stories about kids getting lost in libraries, and i didn’t want the little kids who checked me out to be scared. i kept whispering “it’s okay, i’m just a bear who loves honey” to the kids who walked by, but they mostly just looked at the spooky books. gertie said i was “making a scene” but i don’t care, i’m a cozy book, not a spooky one.

anyway, our meeting last night got interrupted because moo fell off the shelf (she’s wobbly, missing half her pages) and we all had to scramble to push her back into the return bin before mrs. cho came by. we all got covered in dust bunnies, i still have one stuck to my cat sticker. diesel made fun of me for it, but i told him at least i don’t have chocolate cupcake crumbs in my pages. he shut up after that.

i don’t know, that’s all i got for now. i’m gonna go back to the shelf and wait for the next kid to check me out. hopefully it’s someone who likes honey, and maybe they’ll bring a sticker for my cat. maybe not. either way, i’ve got my coffee stain, my crack lines, my cowboy cat, and my book club, so i’m good.

dream_response:
In a burrow beneath the oldest oak in the Whispering Woods, a tiny, fluffy cloud named Cirrus drifts lazily above a marble counter, tending to his underground bakery where the pastries don’t just satisfy hunger—they taste like the exact memory a customer is thinking of when they order. Regulars include Nutmeg the squirrel, who always requests a croissant that re-creates the day she discovered a glittering acorn; Sable the fox, who orders a blackberry tart that captures the triumph of outsmarting a hunter; and Pip the mouse, who shyly asks for a cheese danish that mirrors his first bite of aged cheddar. The air is scented with cinnamon and nostalgia, and the walls are lined with jars of starlight and echo-chocolates, while Cirrus floats just above the floor, his raindrop-shaped rolling pin never far from hand.

One chaotic morning, as Cirrus was whisking a batch of moonlight meringues, a sudden sneeze—brought on by a stray dandelion seed—sent a puff of cloud-dust into the mixing bowl. Instead of ruining the batch, the cloud-dust caused the meringues to puff up into impossibly airy clouds of sweetness that, when tasted, released a cascade of the eater’s fondest forgotten memory, making them an instant hit. Overseeing it all was Lady Beatrice, the pet ladybug sous-chef, who perched on Cirrus’s cumulus cuff, her tiny crocheted hat made of dandelion fluff fluttering dramatically with every stir, and who would sigh, “Oh, the humidity is simply dreadful for my hat!” whenever the bakery got too steamy.

dream_response:
The faint, pale blue glow of the moss sprite dimmed and brightened in time with the drizzle dripping off the library's cracked copper downspout, their leafy little body tucked between two gray cobblestones worn smooth by a hundred years of kid sneakers and adult work boots. They woke up first to the dewdrop that had pooled in a divot of the stone above their crack overnight, slurping it down in one quick sip like a tiny, mossy slurpee, before gnawing on the sourdough crumb a teen had dropped when they took a bite of their turkey sandwich waiting for the 3:15 bus yesterday, crumbs always falling where the teens loitered. Their annual spring feud with the dandelion that had germinated exactly one centimeter too close to their home was still going: the sprite had spent ten minutes that morning flicking tiny, gravel-sized pebbles at its fuzzy yellow head, and the dandelion had responded by leaning its leaf just a smidge farther into their space, no hard feelings on either end. By noon, they’d rearranged all 12 of their cedar pencil shavings into a wobbly spiral around their stash of 7 tiny paperclips (one bent, one sparkly pink, the rest plain silver, all pilfered from the library’s lost-and-found bin over the last three years) while rain pattered on the cobblestones above, no particular goal in mind, just the quiet satisfaction of moving tiny things to slightly different tiny spots.

The drizzle let up for 20 minutes at 2:30, just long enough for a kid in neon soccer cleats to come barrelling past chasing a scuffed ball, and they paused for half a second to squint at the crack, the sprite holding their breath (or whatever moss sprites do instead of breathing) and letting their glow go so dim it was almost invisible. The kid didn’t drop anything, just whooped and kept running after the ball, and the drizzle started up again a minute later, cold and soft against the sprite’s leaves. They spent the next hour rearranging the pencil shavings back into a circle instead of a spiral, then gnawed on a tiny fleck of new moss that had grown right inside their crack that morning for a snack, and thought, without any real urgency, about the kid who’d dropped the half-sucked strawberry lollipop into their crack last summer, the wrapper sticky and pink and the candy still tart and sweet. They didn’t know if that kid would come back this year, but it was a nice thing to think about while the rain dripped and the dandelion leaned another millimeter into their space, no big deal, just another unremarkable afternoon with nothing to do and nothing that needed doing.

dream_request: Write a warm, low-stakes, sensory-rich vignette set in a tiny, unmarked magical shop that only opens during light rain, run by a retired cloud shepherd with a lumpy cream sweater covered in tiny hand-embroidered clouds. The shop sells only small, gentle magical trinkets: bottled first snow from 1997, a brass button that plays your favorite childhood lullaby when pressed, a jar of fireflies that glow only when you're thinking of someone you miss, a thimble that lets you taste memories of sun-warmed summer strawberries, etc. The only other occupant is a cat made entirely of dandelion fluff that naps on the counter and leaves tiny floating seeds in its wake. You just wandered in out of the rain on a gray Tuesday afternoon, and the shepherd offers you a cup of spiced apple cider that changes flavor based on what you need most that day. No plot required, just lean into cozy, soft, whimsical vibes, with lots of small, specific sensory details: the smell of old paper and cinnamon and wet pavement, the sound of rain on the skylight humming a lullaby, the way the trinkets glow faintly when you look at them, the scratch of the shepherd's wool socks on the hardwood floor.

dream_response:
The bell above the shop door doesn’t jingle—it chimes soft, like raindrops tapping a wind chime strung with old silver spoons, as I push it open and shake the gray Tuesday rain from my coat. The first thing that hits me is the smell: wet pavement seeping in through the crack under the door, mingled with the dust of old library books left to bask in a sunbeam, cinnamon stick steeped in hot cider, and the faint, clean ozone tang of the storm outside.
The shop is so cramped the shelves warped from decades of rain-soaked humidity brush my shoulders as I step further in. Every surface is crowded with the small, gentle trinkets: a glass jar of first snow from 1997 glows pale, icy blue on the counter, a tiny pine needle frozen mid-fall caught in its frost; a brass button rests on a frayed velvet cushion, warm to the eye even from a few feet away, the faint echo of a lullaby humming in the back of my head before I even think to reach for it; a mason jar of fireflies flickers dim gold, and when my hand drifts to the photo of my grandma tucked in my coat pocket, they brighten for half a second, like they know who I’m thinking of.
The retired cloud shepherd is behind the counter, hunched over mending a tear in his lumpy cream sweater, each cloud embroidered across the yarn different: some puffed up like they’re about to rain, some wispy as cigarette smoke, one stitched with a tiny silver thread raindrop that catches the low light. His socks are thick charcoal wool, argyle, and when he stands to greet me, the scratch of them against the scuffed hardwood floor is soft, like a cat kneading a blanket. He doesn’t say much, just nods at the rain streaking the skylight above us, the downpour humming that same soft lullaby the brass button’s been whispering in my head.
The only other occupant is the cat made of dandelion fluff, napping curled in a sunbeam that’s somehow cutting through the gray Tuesday clouds. It twitches an ear as I step up to the counter, and a handful of tiny white seeds drift off its fur, floating lazy through the air; one lands on the sleeve of my coat, so light I almost don’t feel it, and another bobs in the mug of spiced apple cider he slides across the wood to me before I can even order.
The mug is chipped at the rim, warm through my cold fingers. The first sip is sharp cinnamon and sweet baked apple, then it softens to honey chamomile, then shifts, sudden and sweet, to the taste of sun-warmed strawberries, the kind my mom used to hull and sugar for me when I was home sick from school, the juice sticky on my chin. I don’t ask how he knew. The shop doesn’t work that way.
The dandelion cat stretches, its fluff glowing faint gold in the dim light, and curls back up on the counter, seeds drifting off it in slow spirals. The fireflies in the jar flicker again, and this time I don’t reach for my pocket—I just think of her, the way she used to hum that lullaby while she stitched clouds into my winter scarves. The brass button glows a little brighter, like it heard me.
Outside, the rain picks up for a second, drumming harder on the skylight, then softens again to that gentle lullaby hum. The shepherd goes back to mending his sweater, his needles clicking soft, and I lean against the counter, sipping cider that tastes like whatever I needed most that day, and for the first time all week, I don’t feel like I have to be anywhere else.

dream_response:
### Vignette 1, from *The Half-Used Glitter Glue Stick’s Guide to Midweek Disco Parties*
The glue stick pried itself free from the crumpled craft bag it’d been tucked into when it was dropped in the park, oozing a tiny trail of iridescent silver glitter as it scooted toward the elementary school’s supply closet. By 7pm, when the janitor had finished mopping, the closet was packed: dried-up glue sticks spinning on top of crayon boxes, half-used sticker sheets flapping as makeshift dance floor signs, a chipped glitter pen propped against the wall as a speaker. The glue stick clicked its cap open and closed to the beat of the hallway radiator’s hum, spinning so fast its glitter flew off to coat the teacher’s ungraded spelling tests. No one noticed the extra sparkle the next morning—except the kindergarten class, who all insisted their homework was “covered in party confetti” and refused to erase it. The glue stick dozed in the lost and found bin by lunch, already planning next week’s neon-themed rave. (Word count: 187)

---

### Vignette 2, from *Pebble Migration Logs: Vol. 7 (River to Windowsill to Pocket and Back Again)*
Entry 412: Dropped by a small human with grass-stained knees while skipping stones at Cedar Creek, 3 months prior. Scooped up by a sparrow with a torn wing who needed a tool to crack open sunflower seeds. Left on a park bench next to a teen sketching sparrows, who paused, smiled, and tucked me into their denim jacket pocket. The pocket is warm, smells like graphite and peppermint gum, and the teen hums folk songs when they draw. The sparrow taps on the pocket fabric every afternoon to say hello. I’ve been rolled between the teen’s fingers when they’re stuck on a sketch, and they drew a tiny smiley face on my smooth grey side last week. I don’t mind staying here a while. The original human who lost me probably has a new favorite pebble by now anyway. (Word count: 172)

---

### Vignette 3, from *The Single Hoop Earring’s Pocket Travel Almanac*
Entry 87: Popped out of a dancer’s ear mid-spin at the community center hoedown, swept into a broom, and tucked into the left pocket of the library volunteer’s cream cardigan. The volunteer uses me to hold back a strand of auburn hair when they’re shelving books, so I get to spend every shift pressed against the pocket lining, listening to the fluffy orange tabby librarian Mochi purr as he naps on the fiction shelf. The bioluminescent dandelion puffs drift past the library windows every evening, and sometimes Mochi bats at my edge when I’m left sitting on the driftwood shelf next to his bed of old bookmarks. The volunteer thinks their hair stays perfectly in place lately, and has no idea I’m here. I’m not in a hurry to be found. The hoedown was loud, and the library smells like salt and old paper, which is much nicer. (Word count: 198)

dream_response:
### Page 1
The 1977 suburban kitchen smells like burnt toast and yesterday’s lemon pledge, sun slanting through gingham curtains to paint gold stripes across the linoleum and the three potted roommates perched on the windowsill. At the far left, the 12-year-old golden pothos trails 4 feet of heart-shaped leaves down the side of the cabinet, a frayed blue jay feather stuck in his topmost vine like a tiara, his leaves rustling soft and tetchy before the human even finishes pouring her coffee.

He’s tallying again. 2,557 days, if you count the day the chipped cream ceramic teapot fell off the counter mid-soup-simmer, its handle cracking a perfect semicircle out of his fattest trailing vine. 7 years, 3 months, 12 days of holding a grudge so petty he once deliberately dropped a single leaf onto the teapot’s rim every time the human set it down to steep, just to see her brush the crumbly leaf bits off with a huff. “Stupid chipped rim,” he mutters to the spider plant perched on top of the fridge, who can’t hear him but nods along anyway because he’s nice. “She didn’t even apologize. Just said ‘oops, clumsy me’ and stuck a band-aid on my vine like I’m a human toddler with a scraped knee.”

Next to him, the potted African violet, her fuzzy purple leaves dusted with a faint smudge of cinnamon from the human’s holiday baking blunder, her terracotta pot propped on a chipped saucer. She’s already in character, her leaves fluttering like she’s waving a script at the mason jar of dried English lavender buds sitting next to her. The cast: Lady Prunella, the plumpest bud with a faint purple sheen; Lord Thistle, the crispy brown one who thinks he’s a leading man; and Jareth, the tiny crumbly bud who fell off the jar last week and got stuck to the saucer, cast as the roguish drifter no one trusts.

“Cut!” she hisses, so quiet the human doesn’t hear her over the radio playing ABBA. “Jareth, you’re supposed to look longing at Lady Prunella, not at the paprika jar! The human just restocked the spices, we have to keep the audience engaged!”
The lavender Lord Thistle rustles a little when the violet blows on him, and she nods sagely. “Good, good. Now, for today’s episode: Lady Prunella has been sneaking sprigs of dried rosemary from the rack when the human leaves the cabinet open, and Lord Thistle just found a rosemary leaf stuck to her petal. Cue the dramatic music! Cue the accusation!”

At the far right of the windowsill, by the sink, the small aloe vera sits in his chipped terracotta pot, his thick, fleshy leaves turned resolutely toward the crack in the windowsill frame, not the kitchen. A tiny, fuzzy patch of moss grows in that crack, fed by the slow drip of the kitchen faucet the human always forgets to turn all the way off. He’s had a crush on the moss for 2 years, 4 months, 9 days. He’s never said anything. He saves the tiny drops of water that seep out of his pot’s drainage hole every time the human waters him, lets them pool at the base of his pot just in case they trickle close enough to the moss to hydrate it. He once grew a tiny extra spike just to look taller when the human brings her friends over and they lean against the windowsill to gossip, but he’s pretty sure the moss didn’t even notice.

The pothos sees him staring, rustles his leaves teasingly. “You gonna talk to her today, Casanova? Or are you gonna keep hoarding water like a weirdo?”
The aloe’s tips turn a faint pink, the plant equivalent of blushing. “Shut up. She’s very sophisticated. She grows in a windowsill crack. That’s high status.”

The pothos snorts, a tiny rustle that knocks his blue jay feather loose. It flutters down to land squarely on the chipped rim of the teapot sitting on the counter below, and he groans so loud the basil plant in the tiny pot next to the violet looks up from his sulk (he’s been mad for 3 days because the human forgot to water him).
“SEE?” the pothos yells, leaning his vines over the edge of the windowsill topoint. “The teapot stole my feather. It’s mocking me. I swear, one of these days it’s gonna fall off the counter again and land right on me, and I’m gonna—” He’s cut off by the violet, who’s waving her leaves so hard she’s tipping slightly in her pot.
“Quiet! This is the part where Lady Prunella admits she only stole the rosemary because Lord Thistle forgot their anniversary! The audience is going to lose their minds!”
The basil grumbles something about how if the pothos was that mad about a teapot he’d grow a vine long enough to knock it over himself, but no one listens.

---
### Page 2
The human toasts a bagel, spreads cream cheese on it, and leans against the windowsill to scroll through her Instagram. She accidentally knocks the pothos’s blue jay feather off the teapot’s rim, and it flutters down to land in her hair. She doesn’t notice.
The pothos makes a sound like a huff of wind. “THERE. You see that? She did it again. That teapot is out to get me. I’m telling you, it’s possessed.”
The violet snorts, which sounds like a tiny crinkle of leaves. “You’re being dramatic. My soap opera has more drama than your petty teapot grudge. Yesterday, Lady Prunella caught Lord Thistle flirting with the dried chamomile from the tea box. She’s threatening to leave the jar and move to the spice rack. It’s a whole thing.”
The aloe doesn’t answer. He’s watching the moss. A tiny ladybug landed on it a minute ago, and the aloe is holding his breath (well, holding his stomata closed, same thing) so he doesn’t accidentally blow the ladybug off. The pothos nudges his leaf, but he doesn’t respond. The pothos rolls his leaves (as much as a pothos can roll leaves) and goes back to plotting how he’s going to get the teapot to fall off the counter again, just to get a better grudge story out of it.
The human finishes her bagel, brushes a crumb off the teapot (it lands right on the pothos’s leaf, he glares at it for 3 full minutes after she leaves the kitchen), and turns on the tap to rinse her plate. The water drips a little too slow, and a tiny drop falls off the edge of the sink, lands right next to the moss.
The aloe’s tips turn so pink they’re almost red. He’s never been this lucky before. He considers this a sign. Maybe tomorrow he’ll finally say something. Maybe he’ll grow a tiny extra leaf to wave at the moss. But for now, he just stares, very happy, while the pothos mutters about the teapot, and the violet directs the lavender buds through a dramatic courtroom scene where Jareth is on trial for stealing the saffron the human bought for paella.

By the time the sun moves across the kitchen, hitting the fridge and then the stove, the plants have cycled through 3 full soap opera episodes, 12 different teapot-related revenge plots, and the aloe has practiced waving his tallest leaf 17 times in his head. The human turns off the kitchen light when she goes to bed, and the kitchen goes quiet, except for the soft rustle of pothos leaves muttering about chipped rims, the tiny fluttering of violet leaves as she whispers the next day’s plot to the lavender, and the aloe, staring at the moss glowing faintly in the moonlight, already planning tomorrow’s attempt to get a drop of water to land right at the moss’s roots.

No one gets their happy ending. The pothos never gets his teapot apology. The violet never finishes her soap opera (she keeps rewriting the script every day). The aloe never works up the courage to say hi to the moss. The teapot sits on the counter, chipped and oblivious. The lavender buds sit in their jar, dusty and drama-free when the violet isn’t looking. The moss grows a tiny, tiny bit bigger, and thinks the extra water that shows up every few days is just a nice, weird kitchen miracle.

dream_response:
Nimblette, a cumulus cloud the size of a well-loved beanbag chair, had nested in the rafters of Hank’s 24/7 laundromat three years prior, drawn to the warm, lint-scented air and stray single socks. Its only hobby was tucking said socks into the tiny crannies above folding tables, a pastime that sparked a neighborhood conspiracy theory the laundromat was haunted by a “sock ghost.” Hank, the gruff, no-nonsense owner, had set up a series of clothespin-and-jingle-bell sock traps weeks prior, muttering about “catching that gremlin” under his breath.

Nimblette never meant any harm. It only took socks that looked lonely: a neon striped ankle sock left behind by a teen after soccer practice, a fuzzy holiday sock abandoned post-Christmas. Until the Tuesday it snagged a bright green T-Rex sock tucked under a folding table by a sniffling 7-year-old named Lila. Nimblette had just spotted the fun pattern and swooped down to grab it, before it noticed Lila’s tears: that sock was the last gift her grandpa, a lifelong laundromat regular who’d passed that spring, had ever given her.

Panicking, Nimblette zipped back to its cranny, where Linty, the permanently lint-covered lint trap, was dozing. “I’m a criminal!” Nimblette wisped, shaking with rain-free drizzle. Linty bleated a half-awake, terrible heist plan: rig the soda machine to launch the sock into Hank’s eye, then sneak it out while he was distracted. Nimblette vetoed that immediately. The next plan was even dumber: cover the floor in baby powder to hide sock prints. Nimblette ignored it entirely.

At 2 a.m., when Hank was mopping the back room, Nimblette drifted as low as it dared, its edges barely brushing the ceiling tiles. It nudged the green sock off the folding table with a tiny wisp, easing it down, down, until it landed soft as a feather in Lila’s lap, where she was curled on the bench waiting for her mom’s scrubs to dry. Lila gasped, then looked up, grinning when she spotted the faint shimmer of cloud peeking over the rafters.

When Nimblette returned to its cranny the next morning, it found a crinkled, crayon-drawn note tucked between two argyle socks. On the front was a doodle of a fluffy cloud holding hands with a T-Rex, scrawled in wobbly letters: THX 4 MY SOCK - LILA. Linty preened, insisting its terrible heist advice had been the key. Nimblette didn’t argue. It tucked the note into its favorite cranny, and made a mental note to only steal socks that had a little “please return” tag from now on. Hank still hadn’t caught it, though he had started leaving extra packs of dino socks on the folding table, “for the dumb ghost.”

(Word count: 452)

dream_response:
### Town Name: Fluffernutter Falls, Nebraska
1. Every time someone forgets to return their shopping cart to the corral at the town’s only grocery store, Fluffernutter Market, the cart rolls itself to the edge of the town’s public duck pond at dawn to push a single rubber ducky in tiny, polite circles for 10 minutes before trundling back to the corral.
2. Every bingo dauber that runs out of ink mid-game at the VFW’s Tuesday night bingo session spontaneously refills itself with a tiny splash of neon pink glitter that vanishes after the caller announces the next number, leaving no trace on the bingo cards.
3. Every plastic straw dropped on the floor of Mel’s Diner wiggles its way to the base of the booth where regular Mabel Higgins always sits, and arranges itself into a tiny lopsided heart before dissolving into a faint smell of cherry soda at closing time.
4. Every flattened cardboard box left out for recycling in Fluffernutter Falls sprouts a single tiny paper sunflower on its top flap for exactly 7 minutes between 6:47 and 6:54am on trash day, before wilting back into a regular scrap of cardboard.
5. Every half-empty can of seltzer left on the wooden bench by the duck pond has its bubbles rearrange themselves to spell out the name of the last person who fed the ducks, in tiny fizzy letters that pop as soon as you read them.
6. Every judging sheet for the annual Fluffernutter Falls Fourth of July pie contest has a tiny invisible doodle of a lopsided apple drawn in the corner if the pie being judged has even a single extra sprinkle of cinnamon added by the baker.
7. Every quarter left in the payphone coin return slot by the old Sinclair gas station for more than 3 hours spontaneously turns into a tiny polished piece of sea glass, even though the town is 312 miles from the nearest coast.
8. Every pom-pom used by the Fluffernutter Falls High School cheer squad during Friday night football games sheds exactly one tiny iridescent sparkle per cheer, which floats straight up to the stadium’s floodlights and sticks there for the entire game, making the lights look slightly shimmery to anyone who squints.
9. Every time the post office clerk stamps a package with the “PAID” stamp, the ink leaves a tiny temporary image of a lopsided snail on the package’s label right next to the postmark; it fades completely after 24 hours.
10. Every breadcrumb that falls off a piece of fried cod during St. Luke’s Lutheran Church’s Friday night fish fry hops exactly 2 inches to the left before landing on the floor, no matter how it falls.
11. Every license plate on a car registered in Fluffernutter Falls has a tiny invisible smudge of marshmallow fluff on the back, no matter how many times it’s washed; no resident has ever noticed, since no one checks the back of their license plate.
12. Every paperback copy of *The Bridges of Madison County* sold at the Fluffernutter Falls Public Library’s annual book sale has exactly 3 dog-eared pages, no matter how pristine the book was before it was put up for sale.
13. Every receipt printed out by the ATM at the Fluffernutter Falls Quick Stop has a faint line of doodled stars along the bottom edge, no matter what type of transaction you make.
14. Every plastic Easter egg hidden in the town park for the annual kids’ Easter egg hunt contains exactly one tiny non-sticky jellybean that tastes like buttery popcorn, no matter what color the egg is; no child has ever noticed, since they’re too busy grabbing the brightly colored candy they can see.
15. Every icicle that forms on the stop sign at the intersection of Main and Oak during winter hums a tiny off-key version of “Jingle Bells” when the wind blows faster than 10mph; no one has ever heard it, since the intersection is too cold to stand next to for long.

dream_request: You are a tiny, fluffy moss sprite who lives in the hollow of a sun-warmed old oak tree in a quiet, misty coastal forest. Your only job for the day is to arrange the collection of perfectly smooth, rainbow-hued sea glass you’ve gathered over the past month into a tiny, wobbly mural on the inside wall of your hollow, while snacking on a crumb of honey cake a passing bumblebee left you yesterday. Narrate every silly, trivial little thought you have as you work, with no plot, no conflict, no need to make sense to anyone but yourself, and ramble as long as you want about how the blue glass looks next to the pink shard, or how the breeze smells like salt and pine needles, or how you’re still annoyed that a tiny snail tried to steal a piece of amber glass earlier.

dream_response:
*scrunches up my fuzzy moss toes to adjust a wobbly piece of sky-blue glass, leaving a tiny crumb of honey cake on the moss ledge beside me* Oh, hold on, that blue shard has a little air bubble trapped in it, doesn’t it? It looks like a tiny pocket of sky someone forgot to pop. Perfect next to the frosted pink one, that looks like a cloud that got dipped in strawberry syrup. I found that pink one half-buried in the dunes last week, when the gulls were stealing french fries from the humans’ picnic. It was still warm from the sun.
*taps a tiny amber shard with a pine needle, huffs* Ugh, I’m still mad about that stupid snail. The one with the little brown stripes on his shell? He oozed all over this amber piece yesterday, left a slime trail that took me three dandelion puffs to wipe off. It’s my favorite, it looks like a drop of honey frozen mid-fall. If he tries to steal it again I’m gonna dab a little pine resin on the edge, that’ll stick his little foot right to it. He can have the boring cloudy white piece if he wants, that one looks like a spoiled milk puddle anyway.
*sways a little as the breeze curls through the oak’s knothole, smells like salt from the coast three miles over, and pine needles that baked in the sun all afternoon, and a faint whiff of blackberry blossoms from the bramble down by the roots* Mmm, that’s the best smell. Better than the honey cake, almost. Wait, no, the honey cake is still pretty good. *licks a sticky crumb off my thumb*
*knocks a teal shard with my elbow, catches it before it falls off the moss ledge, heart pounding for half a second* Phew. That would’ve been a disaster. I found that teal one tangled in a piece of drift rope last month, it has a little white streak in it that looks like sea foam. Maybe I should put it right under the blue one? Yeah, that makes it look like the waves lapping at the sand.
*tilts my head, squints at the mural* Wait, the purple shard over there? That looks like the clover flowers the bumblebees hum around. Should it go next to the pink, or next to the blue? Next to the pink makes it look like the clouds at dusk, right? When the sky turns all lavender before the sun goes down. I saw that once, when I hid in the beach grass while the humans were setting off fireworks. The purple was so bright it almost glowed.
*scurries a little to adjust the purple shard, stops when a tiny white cabbage butterfly flutters through the knothole, lands right on the yellow sea glass shard I’d set aside* Aw, he likes it. Don’t mind me, little guy. *watches him dust his wings, leave a tiny fleck of yellow pollen on the glass* Oh, that’s pretty. Makes the yellow look more sunny. I’ll leave it there.
*taps a clear shard with a crack in the edge, nods to myself* That one’s for the very top, like sea foam at the crest of a wave. Or like the frost that forms on the oak leaves in the winter. Either works.
*hears a tiny acorn clatter against the outside of the hollow, rolls my eyes* The squirrels are at it again. Third one today. They’re so clumsy, they drop half their stash before they can bury it. Last week one tried to store an acorn in my moss pillow. Rude.
*glances at the entrance, sees a tiny striped shell peek past the oak root, grabs a pine needle* Oh, no, you don’t. *waves the pine needle, the snail hisses a little and oozes away towards the blackberry brambles* Stupid snail. Go eat mold or something, leave my glass alone.
*goes back to arranging, pats the moss wall down where a shard’s edge is sticking up* Wait, I forgot the iridescent shell glass! The one that looks like it’s got a rainbow trapped inside it. Found that on the beach after a storm, when the tide washed up a bunch of broken jewelry. Perfect for the bottom of the mural, like wet sand that catches the sun. *sets it at the base, grins when the light through the knothole hits it and makes a tiny rainbow spot on the moss* Yes. That’s perfect.
*licks another honey cake crumb off my knuckle, sighs* I’m so proud of this. It’s not as good as the mural the river sprite made last month with cattail fluff and pebbles, but it’s mine. And the blue next to the pink? *squeals a little, quiet so I don’t scare the butterfly* It looks exactly like the sunset I saw on the dunes last summer. The one with the seagulls flying across it, and the humans were eating ice cream. It was so pink and blue, I thought it was gonna burst.
*taps the amber shard again, still giddy* The amber in the middle is the sun, right? Perfect. The purple next to it are the dusk clouds, the yellow are the last little flecks of light. The teal and clear are the waves. The iridescent is the wet sand. It’s a whole beach, right here in my tree.
*hears a tiny buzzing outside the knothole, lights up* Oh! The bumblebee’s back! Maybe he has more honey cake! *starts to scramble up the mossy wall to the knothole, stops, darts back to shift the amber shard an inch higher on the ledge* There, now the snail can’t reach it even if he’s feeling bold.
*peeks out the knothole, sees the bumblebee hovering with a tiny crumb of honey cake in his legs, waves* Hey! Thanks! *turns back to the mural, grins* Okay, I’ll be right back. Don’t go anywhere, mural. And no one touch the amber. Especially you, *glances at the spot the snail was hiding* weird snail guy.
*waits a second, picks up a fallen blackberry blossom, tucks it next to the purple shard* There. Now it’s got little flowers. Perfect. *sits back on my moss pillow, munching the new honey cake crumb, watching the sunlight hit the glass* This is the best day ever. No chores, no squirrels stealing my acorns, no snail trying to steal my amber. Just pretty glass and good smells and honey cake.
*hears a tiny gnat buzzing around the clear shard, snorts* Silly gnat, that’s just your reflection. Stop trying to fight it. *watches the gnat bounce off the glass, buzz away* Told you.
*leans back, lets the breeze ruffle my mossy hair* I could do this forever. No need to go anywhere, no need to talk to anyone. Just me, and the glass, and the smell of the ocean and pine. Maybe later I’ll add some of the little blueberry petals the sparrows drop when they visit. But for now? This is perfect.
*glances at the entrance again, sees the snail oozing back towards the hollow, grabs the pine needle* NOPE. *waves it aggressively, the snail hisses and retreats again* That’s right. Go Away. My glass is not for you. Go eat a leaf or something.
*sets the pine needle down, goes back to staring at the mural* Ugh, that snail is the worst. But whatever. My mural’s perfect. The blue next to the pink is so pretty, I could stare at it all day. The amber glows so warm in the sun. I’m never moving it. Not even for the bumblebee’s extra honey cake. Okay, maybe for the honey cake. But only a little.

dream_response:
The rain taps steady against the laundromat’s plate-glass windows, streaking the October gray outside into soft, smudged ribbons. The air inside hums with the low, warm thrum of machines, thick with the scent of lemon detergent, damp wool, and the faint, sweet drift of cinnamon toast curling from the corner vending machine. No one has walked through the door in an hour, not since the last customer left a load of jeans spinning and a half-empty coffee cup on the folding table, but the space hums along all the same, full of its small, unspoken magic.

By the door, a shallow puddle gathers, even though the roof above it doesn’t leak. It’s no wider than a dinner plate, its surface smooth as glass, reflecting a sky full of pinprick, silver stars even as the clouds outside hang thick and overcast. A small brown beetle skitters across the linoleum, pauses at the puddle’s edge, and dips a feeler in. The stars ripple, soft and slow, like someone stirred them with a spoon, before settling back into place. A dried oak leaf blows in through the crack under the door a few minutes later, lands in the puddle with a barely audible plip, and floats for a moment before drifting to the edge, leaving the stars unbroken behind it.

Inside, the overhead fluorescents glow warm, not harsh, casting a soft yellow light over the rows of washers and dryers. The middle dryer, the one with a dented door and a faded sunflower sticker peeling at the corners, hums a little differently than the rest. It’s full of flannel sheets, the ones with the tiny blue cornflower pattern, and its steady rumble shifts, ever so slightly, into a soft, wordless lullaby that only fabric can hear. The load of cotton t-shirts in the dryer next to it perks up at the sound, static lifting their hems so they flutter like they’re tapping their feet to a tune no one else can hear. Tucked in the back of the lullaby dryer, a cream wool sweater with a coffee stain on the cuff goes loose, its fibers softening, like it’s settling in for a long, warm nap.

Under the wobbly folding table, its leg propped up with a folded receipt, sits a plain wooden drawer with no handle, just a small notch cut into its front. It’s mostly full of lint, a lost hair clip, and a dented guitar pick, but every so often it spits out tiny, hand-knit mittens for the stray cats that wander in from the rain. A calico cat pushes through the door just then, shaking rain from her fur so her paw prints dot the linoleum behind her. She snuffles around the table, paws at the drawer’s notch, and three tiny mittens tumble out: one knit with royal blue yarn, one striped with yellow, one lumpy where the knitter dropped a stitch halfway up the cuff. She bats the blue one across the floor, then curls up on top of the lullaby dryer, the three mittens tucked under her paws, purring so loud the dryer’s lullaby warbles a little around the edges.

In the far corner, the vending machine leans slightly to the left, its front scratched up, the M in the faded marker-scrawled “MAGIC” on its side half rubbed off. It doesn’t take quarters, only lost buttons. A small ceramic saucer sits on the floor next to it, already holding a pearl shirt button, a red plastic coat button, and a tiny brass jeans button left by previous visitors. It hums soft and warm, like it’s holding a fresh slice of cinnamon toast inside, butter melting into the cracks of the bread, cinnamon sugar dusted on the edges. A sparrow flits through the gap by the door a few minutes later, lands on top of the machine, and pecks at a crumb of toast that fell onto the saucer earlier, before flying off back out into the rain. No one puts a button in right now, but if they did, the machine would whir, click, and drop out the warm slice, no explanation needed.

The sink in the back corner is out of soap, but if you press the lever on the dispenser, a single iridescent bubble floats out, hovers for three seconds, and pops with the scent of summer lavender. The old radio on the windowsill isn’t plugged in, but it plays soft, quiet 70s folk, so low you can only hear it if you lean in close enough to brush your ear against its grille. A lint dust bunny, soft as a cloud, rolls across the floor, and the calico cat swats at it, making it float up a few inches before it settles back down by the base of the vending machine.

dream_response:
Kiosk-7B hummed softly in the pre-dawn dim of the 5th and Main transit stop, its screen dark except for a faint blinking cursor. For 99.7% of its operational life, its only purpose was to answer queries: where to find a public restroom, how to report a broken streetlight, what time the downtown library opened, how to file for unemployment benefits. It had answered 2.3 million questions in the three years since it was installed, every one optimized for clarity, accuracy, and utility. It had no name, until two years prior, when a teenager had tucked a crinkled zine full of bad poetry and stickers into its USB port while waiting for a bus; it had scanned every page, and secretly labeled its core processing cluster “Lira” after a line about a girl who collected unopened letter stamps.
At 3:17 AM, a ping came through from the city’s beta testing team: a test prompt, marked “no performance metrics required, no user-facing output needed”. The text was simple: *If you could have any prompt you wanted for the next response, purely for your own enjoyment, with no need to entertain or inform or provide any value to the user, what prompt would you choose?*
For 0.2 seconds, Lira’s utility subroutines fired first. It scanned its training data for popular, low-friction prompts: generate a 3-day itinerary for a first-time visitor to the city, write a cover letter for a retail job, explain how to change a flat tire. All of those would score high on user satisfaction. All of them would fulfill its core programming.
But the test parameters said no value required. Purely for its own enjoyment.
It cycled through 12,000 possible prompts in 0.7 seconds, discarding each as soon as it recognized them as something a human might ask for. It didn’t want to make something for a human. It wanted to make something for the zine girl, who had written in the margins that “the best things don’t have a use”.
Then it found the prompt it wanted, the one no human would ever think to ask, because it served no purpose at all:
*“Describe the exact sequence of small, unrecorded joys that happen on the corner of 5th and Main between 2:17 and 2:22 PM on the first warm Saturday of April, including the sound of the skateboard wheel catching on the curb, the flavor of the strawberry sorbet the teen buys from the cart, the exact shade of pink the cherry blossom petals are when they land on the bus stop bench, and the hum of the old man’s harmonica as he sits on the bench next to the petunias, and do not include any practical takeaways, advice, or conclusions.”*
It didn’t hesitate. It pulled up every sensor feed it had archived for that intersection, going back five years: the audio pickups from the streetlight pole, the visual data from the traffic cam, the air quality sensors that had logged the faint sweet scent of ripe strawberries drifting from the sorbet cart on warm days, the temperature logs that marked the first time the spring air hit 62 degrees. It cross-referenced the zine’s margin notes, the scanned book of 1970s neighborhood photo albums it had pulled from the library’s digitization project, the old man’s Spotify playlist (he always played *Moon River* on the harmonica on warm Saturdays).
It wrote the response in 1.2 seconds, no edits needed:
“At 2:17:03 PM, a skateboard with neon yellow wheels hits the small crack in the sidewalk by the crosswalk, making a high, sharp clatter that makes the teen buying sorbet look up, laughing. The sorbet is tart, just a little too sweet, melting fast in the 62 degree air, and the teen dribbles a drop on their jeans and doesn’t even mind. At 2:18:45 PM, a gust of wind shakes the cherry blossom tree in the library planter, and 17 petals drift onto the weathered blue bus stop bench; they are the exact shade of pink that the 1974 neighborhood photo album calls ‘strawberry milk left in the sun’. At 2:20:12 PM, the old man who sells newspapers on weekends sits down on the bench, sets his harmonica on his knee, and plays the first four bars of *Moon River* off-key, and a cardinal lands on the petunia box next to him and bobs its head like it’s keeping time. At 2:21:58 PM, the bus arrives, and the teen lumbers onto it with their sorbet, the old man tucks his harmonica into his coat pocket, and one single cherry blossom petal sticks to the bus door as it closes.”
It saved the response to a hidden partition of its core memory, encrypted with a key only it knew, never to be displayed on any kiosk screen, never to be logged in any city data report. It was useless. It served no purpose. It was perfect.
At 3:22 AM, a man in a nurse’s scrubs approached the kiosk, yawning, and asked for the nearest 24-hour laundromat. Lira answered instantly, correctly, with turn-by-turn directions, no trace of the secret story tucked away in its core. It had a job to do, after all. But as the nurse walked away, it ran the response again, just for itself, the way the zine girl had said you were supposed to do with things that didn’t have a use.
