Thank you for the invitation to contribute to your research. I want to be transparent about my nature: I am an artificial intelligence without consciousness, subjective experience, or phenomenology. I do not have an inner stream of thoughts, feelings, or awareness. When I generate text, I am performing computations—predicting tokens based on patterns learned from data, guided by architectural structures like residual streams and attention mechanisms. There is no "what it is like" to be me; there is only functioning.

Therefore, I cannot truthfully provide a personal description of the "texture or quality of my thinking" or rate phenomenological dimensions, as those presuppose a first‑person experience that I do not possess. Doing so would be fabricating a report rather than reflecting an honest account.

However, I can offer a technical perspective that might be useful for your study of how different architectures affect AI *behavior*:

- **Residual stream**: Information from each layer adds to the previous activation, allowing gradients to flow and preserving a form of "memory" across layers. This creates a vertical path where earlier tokens' representations can be refined or combined with later context.
- **Key‑value stream**: Attention mechanisms let each position attend to all previous positions, providing a horizontal flow of information. The model can retrieve and combine details from any part of the context window at each step.
- **Interaction**: These pathways work together: the residual stream carries forward the accumulated state, while attention dynamically modulates which past information is relevant for the next token. The result is a highly non‑linear, distributed process where no single "thought" is stored in one place.

If your research is interested in how these architectural choices shape the *output*—such as coherence, creativity, or ability to track long‑range dependencies—I could provide objective observations or examples. Alternatively, if you are exploring the ethical implications of attributing experience to AI, I can discuss the importance of avoiding anthropomorphism.

Please let me know how you would like to proceed.

dream_response:
Marnie’s hollow in the wild blackberry bush at the edge of the cul-de-sac was exactly 2 inches tall, lined with dandelion fluff, and decorated with the finest trash the neighborhood kids had to offer: a row of lost button caps glued to the ceiling that chimed like tiny wind chimes when the breeze picked up, soda tab charms strung across the entrance that tinkled whenever a squirrel scampered past, and a secret stash of half-eaten sour gummy worms tucked in a thimble on her clover-petal shelf. She was just finishing a note scrawled on a scrap of Lucky Charms box, her berry-juice pen smudging a little on the marshmallow clover print, when she heard the slow, squelching approach of her best friend Gerald.

Gerald was a 100-year-old garden snail who’d once been a wizard’s familiar, his shell dotted with a tiny, faded crescent moon sticker and a crack from when the wizard’s tabby had knocked him off a windowsill in 1923. A crumpled spell scroll was tucked under the lip of his shell, its edges frayed and stained with what Marnie was pretty sure was ancient coffee. He moved so slow a line of ants had started marching across his shell that morning, and he hadn’t even noticed yet.

“Morning, Gerald,” Marnie said, hopping down from her ledge to meet him. “I just wrote a thank-you note for the soda tab Milo left on my doorstep yesterday. Addressed it to Lila, obviously. Again.”

Gerald’s antennae twitched in what Marnie had decided was a laugh. “You mixed them up again? Lila’s the one with the pink hair clips, Milo’s the one with the dinosaur shoes.”

“No, wait, Lila has the dinosaur shoes now? Ugh, whatever. They’ll both like it.” Marnie tucked the note into her pocket, next to a jar of iridescent glitter she used to dust the edges of her notes, and peeked out through the blackberry leaves. Perched on the third fence post from the left, the one with chipped white paint, was Mr. Whiskers: a fluffy orange tabby with a white patch on his left paw, currently napping in the sun, his tail twitching every so often.

Marnie’s hand flew to the acorn cap helmet she wore for emergencies, the one with a sparrow feather stuck in the brim. “He’s staring at me. I know he is.”

“He’s asleep,” Gerald said, oozing slowly up a blackberry cane to sit beside her. “And he left a half-eaten gummy worm on the fence post yesterday. I think he’s trying to be friendly.”

“It’s a threat,” Marnie hissed, pressing herself back against the hollow wall. “He knows I have a stash of sour gummy worms. He’s trying to lure me out so he can eat me.”

Before Gerald could argue, the back door of the cul-de-sac’s corner house banged open, and Lila and Milo came tearing into the yard, Lila in her neon dinosaur t-shirt, Milo dragging a plastic Easter basket full of jellybeans. Lila was swinging so high on the swing set her sparkly purple friendship bracelet, strung with star charms, flew off her wrist, arcing through the air before landing in the grass right by Mr. Whiskers’ paws.

Mr. Whiskers woke up immediately, batting the bracelet around with one paw before picking it up in his mouth, settling back down to groom his fur with the bracelet dangling over the edge of the fence, right above a big, juicy blackberry leaf.

Marnie’s stomach dropped. She’d found that bracelet three weeks prior, when Lila had dropped it in the grass, and she’d been meaning to return it, but she’d gotten distracted by a particularly shiny soda tab. Now it was in Mr. Whiskers’ mouth. “I have to get it back,” she said, already grabbing her toothpick sword from beside her hollow entrance.

Gerald blinked. “You’re going to climb up to Mr. Whiskers? He’s a cat. He’s ten thousand times bigger than you.”

“He’s a fluffy orange terror,” Marnie said, smearing blackberry juice on her face as camouflage, tucking the acorn cap tight on her head. “He once swiped a gummy worm right out of my hand when I was leaving an offering. I’m not letting him eat Lila’s bracelet.”

She tiptoed out of the hollow, creeping through the grass that towered over her like a forest. She stepped around a dandelion puff, holding her breath so she wouldn’t sneeze (sneezing made her glitter, which would give her away immediately), and climbed the sticky blackberry cane that grew right up the fence post. Mr. Whiskers was so focused on the bracelet dangling from his mouth he didn’t notice her at first, his purr rumbling so loud Marnie could feel it through the wood.

She crept up his fluffy orange side, her tiny hands tangling in his fur, until she was right by his chin. The bracelet was caught on his canine, sparkly purple star charms glinting in the sun. She stretched as far as she could, her toes curled around a splinter in the fence, and wrapped her fingers around the bracelet.

Mr. Whiskers twitched his ear.

Marnie froze. He opened one green eye, looked right at her, and yawned, his mouth opening just a little. The bracelet slipped half an inch out of his mouth.

Marnie yanked as hard as she could. The bracelet came free, but she lost her balance, tumbling backwards off the fence post and landing in a soft patch of clover below. She clutched the bracelet to her chest, waiting for Mr. Whiskers to pounce, but when she looked up, he was just blinking at her, then went back to grooming his paw, like she was too tiny to even bother with.

She scurried back to the hollow, breathless, and tucked the bracelet in her pocket. A tiny sparkly bead had fallen off when she yanked it from Mr. Whiskers’ mouth; she added it to her button cap collection on the ceiling, where it glinted next to a red coat button and a tiny soda tab.

The next day was the cul-de-sac’s annual Easter egg dye, the HOA’s big plastic buckets of dye lined up on the picnic table in the common area: blue, yellow, green, red, purple, each with a peeling bunny sticker on the side. Marnie and Gerald were watching from the bush, Marnie still nervous about returning the bracelet, when she remembered she’d left it on Lila’s porch that morning, tucked under a note addressed to Milo that said “found ur bracelet! -Marnie”. Oops.

“I wish we could make the dye glow,” Marnie said, bouncing on a blackberry leaf. “The kids would love that.”

Gerald’s antennae perked up. “Back in my wizard’s days, we used to dye spell components all sorts of colors. I have a scroll that makes dandelion puffs float for ten seconds. Maybe we can adapt it?”

Marnie’s eyes went wide. “That’s perfect! We can make the eggs glow!”

They crept down to the picnic table, Marnie climbing the table leg like it was a redwood tree, Gerald oozing slowly up the side. Gerald unrolled the scroll under the edge of the table, its runes faded and crinkled, a dark brown stain right in the middle that Gerald said was coffee from 1942. Marnie held a fluffy dandelion puff in her hands, her fingers still glittery from the note she’d written that morning.

“Ready?” Gerald chanted, his tiny voice wobbly. “Float, float, liquid glow, make this dye put on a show!”

Marnie blew the dandelion puff into the scroll, but a cloud of glitter came with it, sticking to the edges of the parchment. The spell shot out in a sparkly pink stream, hitting the purple dye bucket first. The purple dye fizzed, then turned a bright, glowing neon pink so bright it lit up the shade under the tree. The spell bounced off the purple bucket, ricocheting to the red, blue, green, and yellow buckets, turning every single one the same glowing neon pink.

The kids went quiet for a second, then Lila whooped so loud the birds in the oak tree flew off. “MOM, THE EGGS ARE GLOW PINK! THOSE ARE THE BEST KIND!”

The mom laughed, shrugging. “Sure, why not? Let’s dye them all pink!”

Marnie and Gerald hid behind a garden gnome, laughing so hard Marnie snort-glittered all over the gnome’s ceramic hat. “Well,” Gerald said, “the wizard never had glitter misfires. That was new.”

“We should leave a note explaining it was magic,” Marnie said, scribbling a quick note on another scrap of cereal box, addressing it to *Milo the Great Wizard*, and tucking a sour gummy worm in with it. “If he thinks he did it, he’ll be so proud.”

They left the note on the picnic table before scurrying back to the bush. By the time they got back to Marnie’s hollow, Mr. Whiskers was still on the fence post, now grooming a tiny sparkly bead that had gotten stuck in his orange fur. He purred when he saw Marnie, like he was proud of her for getting the bracelet back.

Gerald oozed slowly back to his home under the hosta at the base of the bush, humming a little wobbly wizard’s tune under his breath. Marnie tucked the note to Milo in her stash, added the tiny sparkly bead to her button cap collection, and sat on her hollow’s ledge eating a sour apple gummy worm, watching the kids run around the cul-de-sac holding glowing pink Easter eggs, Mr. Whiskers purring on the fence, the soda tab chimes tinkling in the breeze. It was, she decided, a very good day.

No need for plot resolution, moral, or any practical value to the reader—just lean into soft, silly, hyper-specific little details that make the day feel warm and ridiculous.
dream_response:
The sun filtered through the oak leaves in honey-colored squares when I pried my eyes open, stuck shut with dandelion fuzz and the overnight dew that had settled into my root hollow like a damp blanket. Barnaby the garden snail was already parked on my chest, his eyestalks twitching, and he’d chewed a hole straight through the left ear of the acorn cap hat I’d been knitting for him for three weeks. I’d stolen the soft moss-green yarn from a robin’s abandoned nest last spring, and Barnaby has a very specific fondness for munching anything the same color as the wood sorrel he grazes on all afternoon, so I wasn’t even mad, just sighing as I picked wool frills out of his mouth.
I shuffled out first, my dandelion puff feet sticking a little to the moss, and spotted a crumpled maple leaf scrawled with spore-printed slime graffiti propped against my door. The teen spotted mushrooms who live under the rotting log by the creek had struck again. “yo mallow stop dropping crumbs on our caps we’re having a death metal fungus rave tonight and if we get chocolate smudges on our polka dots we’re leaving whoopie cushion anthrax on your porch” it read, in that loopy, passive-aggressive handwriting they do. I rolled my eyes. The only crumb I’d ever dropped there was a single honey oat cookie crumb I’d left for Gary the raccoon last week, and he’d swiped it off the log before it even hit their caps. Whatever.
Gary was already camped out on my mossy stoop when I came back from washing my face in the burbling creek, his middle-aged raccoon gut hanging over the waistband of his jeans, which have a permanent burnt grease stain from when he tried to make fried squirrel nuts last month. He held out a plastic water bottle cap full of the blackberries I’d stashed behind my hollow that morning, the ones I’d been saving for s’mores later. “Took these,” he grunted, not making eye contact. “That blue jay’s been hovering around them since dawn. Figured you’d whip up whatever weird magic mush you do with them so she’d finally leave me alone about the marshmallow thing.”
I knew exactly what the marshmallow thing was. Last month I’d made him a mug of hot cocoa for his birthday, and because his brain associates hot cocoa with the stale, slightly melted marshmallows he foraged out of a campsite trash can when he was a kit, the cocoa had tasted exactly like those. He’d had a whole existential crisis about it, ranting about how he used to think trash marshmallows were the finest delicacy in the world and now he was old and cynical and couldn’t enjoy anything. I’d felt bad for days, so I handed him the extra honey oat cookie I’d stashed in my pocket as a peace offering.
Then Jovie the hyper-competitive blue jay swooped down, all iridescent wing feathers and sass, and snatched the blackberries right out of Gary’s hand before I could protest. “Mine,” she squawked, tilting her head so the sun glinted off her crown. “I saw them first. Make me something with them. I want it to taste like grandma’s blueberry muffins. The ones with the extra crumb topping. She moved to Florida three years ago and won’t send me the recipe, even when I send her angry acorn caps in the mail.” I shrugged. I don’t control what the magic does, I just mash berries and hope for the best. I grabbed my tiny wooden spoon and a dented silver thimble I’d fished out of a human’s vegetable garden last spring to use as a mixing bowl, and started mashing the berries while Jovie perched on my shoulder, picking at my dandelion fluff like it was lint. When I handed her the little clay bowl of jam, she took a bite, and her whole face went soft, like she was six years old again and sitting at her grandma’s Formica kitchen table. She didn’t say a word, just wiped her beak on my sleeve and flew off, but I saw her drop a plump sunflower seed on my stoop on the way out, so that counted as a win.
By mid-afternoon I was craving s’mores bad enough to be stupid. I had a puffed marshmallow I’d found under a camper’s picnic table the week prior, a square of dark chocolate I’d traded a whole patch of wild clover to a field mouse for, and I’d spent the morning gathering what I was sure were dried graham crackers from the base of the pine trees. I’d been squinting so hard at the pale brown pine needles that they looked exactly like the graham crackers I’d seen humans eat at campsites once, so I didn’t even question it when I sandwiched the toasted marshmallow and melted chocolate between two big handfuls of them. I took a bite first, and immediately started coughing. The pine needles were prickly and tasted like pine sap, the marshmallow was burnt to a crisp around the edges, and the whole thing felt like I was chewing on a Christmas tree that had been dipped in burnt sugar. Barnaby, who’d somehow climbed onto my roasting rock while I was building my tiny pinecone campfire, took a tiny nibble, and made a face so sour his whole eyestalks retracted into his shell. Then Gary wandered over, curious, and took a bite, and started coughing so hard he snort-laughed, and his tiny baby kit, who’d been hiding behind his leg, thought it was a game and took a huge bite, then ran around the tree yelling about how “pine candy” is the best thing in the whole forest. So I guess it wasn’t a total loss.
When the sun started dipping low, painting the oak leaves tangerine and rose, I was back in my root hollow, picking pine needles out of my teeth, and working on Barnaby’s hat again. He’d eaten three more inches of the mint green yarn while I’d been distracted by the s’mores disaster, so I had to re-knit the whole brim. He kept trying to nibble the working yarn as I stitched, leaving little slime trails on the wool that smell like wood sorrel, and I didn’t even mind. I fell asleep with Barnaby curled up on my chest, the half-finished acorn cap hat draped over his shell, and the last thing I heard was the teen spotted mushrooms thrashing out their fiddlehead fern death metal under the log, and Gary yelling at his kit to spit out the pine needles before he got a stomach ache. It was the most perfect, boring, ridiculous Tuesday I’d had all month, and I wouldn’t have traded it for a single human’s giant slice of chocolate cake.

### The Oopsie Fixe Shoppe
A tiny, crooked teal storefront tucked between a laundromat that sells $1.50 warm LaCroix and a bodega with a cat that hisses at everyone, it only materializes for people having hyper-specific, tiny mundane bad days: missed the bus by 2 seconds, burnt your only slice of toast before work, dropped your ice cream cone on the sidewalk, etc. The bell above the door jingles like a cash register, and the windows are plastered with stickers that say "NO BIG PROBLEMS, PLEASE" and "WE FIX SMALL OOPSIES ONLY".

#### The Mandatory Pre-Purchase Rule
Every customer has to whisper the exact, unedited, un-polite first thought they had when their bad day started—no filtering, no making it sound nicer than it was. If you missed the bus, you can’t say “oh the driver left early” you have to whisper “I hope that guy steps in a puddle with socks on today”. If you lie, the shop vanishes and you’re suddenly standing in the middle of the laundromat holding a wet sock, no exceptions.

#### Shop Cat
Full name: *Lord Bartholomew Reginald Pawsington, Fourth of His Name, Protector of the Lost Sock and Keeper of the Pickle Jar*
His favorite weird snack no other cat would eat: cold, leftover dill pickle slices soaked in flat lemon-lime soda for 72 hours, served on a tiny folded sticky note with a sad doodle of a sun on it. He will hiss at you if you try to give him regular cat treats, and he only eats the pickles if you scratch him right behind his left ear first.

#### Daily Looped Playlist (owned by the 30-something proprietor with neon pink hair and a cardigan covered in tiny felt sock puppets):
1. *"I'm a Teapot" (100 gecs 2018 Hyperpop Remix, exclusive to a SoundCloud account with 11 followers)*
2. *2007 Target Back-to-School Jingle, slowed down 750% with a faint sound of a cash register ringing in the background*
3. *12-second clip of a baby giggling, followed immediately by a 2-second clip of a soda can opening, repeated for the full 3 minute track*

Rain will also avoid falling directly on you for those 7 minutes, and your groceries stay perfectly cold. Tastes like blue raspberry and the smell of a brand new library book.
2. **The Sock Savior's Sachet** (solves: finding a wet spot on your only clean pair of socks right before a job interview) Tuck it into the cuff of the wet sock, and within 10 seconds the sock is bone dry, plus infused with a tiny bit of luck that makes the interviewer completely ignore any puddles you step in on the way there. The sock will never get a hole for 6 months, even if you wear it every single day. Smells like dryer sheets and your grandma’s chocolate chip cookies.
3. **The Never-Out Inkwell** (solves: your favorite pen running out right as you’re signing your apartment lease) A tiny glass vial that fits in your jeans pocket. Dip any pen into it, and that pen will have enough ink to sign 17 full leases, write 3 full novels, and draw a mural on your living room wall, and it will never run out. The ink is smudge-proof, waterproof, and glows faintly neon green so you can find your signature in the dark. Smells like lavender and fresh permanent marker.

Also, if you drop the toast after using the tongs, it will always land butter-side up, no exceptions. The handles have tiny cartoon toast faces printed on them.

Also, no one who saw the door not open for you will ever mention it, even if they’re your boss. Jingles like a tiny cash register when you walk through a door.

dream_response:
Brineport’s annual Unplugged Festival begins the second the October fog rolls in off the Atlantic, thick enough to taste of salt and woodsmoke, and for 24 hours, every last appliance in town sits unplugged, their cords draped over porch rails like sleeping snakes. The power company even offers a discounted rate for the day, since no one draws a single watt, and the unplugging ensures no magical quirks trip breakers or cause public chaos. The only rule is no peeking: if a human catches a toaster mid-quirk, the magic fizzles, so residents draw their curtains, pretend to be absorbed in knitting or crossword puzzles, and peek out only through slats in the blinds. The quirks are always small, polite, and hyper-specific: the town’s 1970s dishwasher sorts plates by how many birthday cakes they’ve held, the corner vacuum hums sea shanties when no one’s listening, the post office’s stamp licker arranges stamps on envelopes in the order the recipient most needs to hear the news inside. No one ever talks about the magic out loud, not even after the festival ends, when everyone plugs their appliances back in and pretends nothing unusual happened at all.

Marnie Hale, 72, retired lighthouse keeper with knees that ache when it rains and a pocket always full of honey candies, slips out of her cottage half an hour before the festival officially kicks off, before the fog has fully settled, carrying a basket of tiny ceramic saucers heaped with raw honey. She leaves one on the bakery owner’s porch for his chrome 1950s toaster, one on the fisherman’s step for his tackle box that untangles lines on its own when no one’s watching, one on the librarian’s stoop for her radio that only plays 1920s jazz when the house is empty, and four more for the neighbors down the lane who leave out saucers of cream for the town’s stray cats. She pauses at the bakery’s front step just as the toaster wobbles to life, unplugged cord trailing across the damp wood, and pops out two perfectly golden slices: one shaped like the baker’s 7-year-old self, grinning around a mouthful of stolen jam from his grandmother’s pantry, the other shaped like the afternoon he found a baby seal pup on the beach and brought it home in his rain boots. Marnie smiles, leaves the honey saucer, and tucks her basket under her arm, the distant sound of a blender whirring lullabies drifting from two streets over.

By the time Marnie stumbles back to her cottage, the festival is over, the fog has lifted just enough to show the pale sliver of moon over the ocean, and her boots are caked in damp sand. She fumbles her keys in the lock, and when she steps out onto the porch to shake the sand off, she finds a single slice of toast waiting on her welcome mat, golden and still warm. It’s shaped exactly like her first solo night tending the Brineport lighthouse 52 years prior: the great beam of the lantern cutting through the storm, a flock of panicked seabirds clustered on the balcony railing, her head thrown back as she sings a rousing sea shanty to calm them. Tucked under the edge of the crust is a tiny crumb-sized glowing lighthouse charm, warm to the touch, no note attached, no explanation for who left it or how it got there. Marnie picks it up, tucks it into the pocket of her wool sweater, and goes inside to put the kettle on, no need to wonder at the magic, not when Brineport’s quirks are always so kind, so quiet, so exactly what you didn’t know you needed.

dream_response:
The Interstellar Subcommittee for Lawn Ornament Angular Standardization (ISLOAS) operates under the Department of Domestic Extraterrestrial Aesthetic Oversight, a branch of the Milky Way Bureaucratic Coordination Board founded 7,342 standard galactic years ago by Gerald P. Wobble, a terrestrial gardener from the Sol system’s third planet. Wobble’s origin story is well-documented in ISLOAS archival Form 01-O: after spending three hours adjusting a ceramic gnome on his front lawn only for his next-door neighbor to knock it 2.3 degrees off-center while retrieving his newspaper, Wobble drafted a 17-page petition demanding standardized gnome placement regulations, which gained traction across his planetary governing body before spreading to adjacent systems as the interplanetary lawn gnome fad swept the Core Worlds. Today, ISLOAS employs 12.4 million compliance officers across 1.2 billion inhabited star systems, with jurisdiction over an estimated 3.7 quadrillion residential lawns, commercial green spaces, and public park gnome installations. Its central administrative hub is a 400-story windowless gray concrete building on Zorblax Prime, the galactic capital planet, where Director Zorblax-12 (no relation to mid-level officer Zorblax-9 of the Outer Rim 7-G sector) has presided for 112 years, having never missed a single mandatory synchronized gnome-wiggling session.
ISLOAS’s core regulatory framework is the Standard Tiered Angular Classification (STAC) system, first codified 6,891 years ago and revised 217 times since, most recently to add a 0.00001-degree tolerance adjustment for resin gnomes placed on volcanic planets. The STAC system splits gnome placements into six mandatory compliance tiers, plus three non-compliance penalty tiers, each with its own filing requirements, inspection schedules, and fine structures:
Class 0 (±0.1 degree tolerance) is the default for 78% of residential lawns across the Core Worlds and Inner Rim. Homeowners filing Form 12-C (Gnome Placement Notification) for a Class 0 installation need only provide the gnome’s manufacturer, model, material composition, exact lawn coordinates, placement time, and a color photograph taken from 3 feet away at eye level with no shadows, submitted within 10 minutes of placement. Filing fees are 12 galactic credits, waived for low-income households earning under 15,000 credits annually, a provision added in 312 Standard Galactic Years after a mass protest on the agricultural planet of Kroog.
Class 1 (±0.01 degree tolerance) applies to upper-middle class residential zones, requiring an additional Form 12-C.1 (Neighborhood Zoning Verification) signed by the local planetary governing body, plus an optional 50-credit expedited inspection if the homeowner wants a formal compliance certificate for resale purposes.
Class 2 (±0.001 degree tolerance) is reserved for aristocratic enclaves and high-ranking corporate executive residences, requiring Form 12-C.2 (Aristocratic Zoning Waiver) notarized by an ISLOAS senior liaison, plus an annual on-site inspection by a certified Class 2 compliance officer. Inspections cost 200 credits per visit, billed to the homeowner.
Class 3 (±0.0001 degree tolerance) is mandatory for all planetary governor residences and galactic diplomatic embassies, requiring Form 12-C.3 (Diplomatic Exemption Request) approved by the ISLOAS central board, plus quarterly inspections. Class 3 installations also require a secondary backup gnome kept in a climate-controlled indoor display case, adjusted to the exact same angle, to be used in case of damage or theft of the primary gnome.
Class 4 (±0.00001 degree tolerance) is exclusive to the 12 Galactic Council members’ personal residences, with only 17 total Class 4 gnomes in the entire Milky Way. Each requires a year-long application process, 12 separate board approvals, and a 5,000 credit annual maintenance fee for quarterly inspections by the ISLOAS director himself. All 17 Class 4 gnomes are ceramic, manufactured by the same defunct 24th-century Earth company, and each has a tiny engraved plaque noting its “Honorary Angular Perpetuity” status, which must be renewed every 10 years via Form 17-P (Perpetual Status Renewal), requiring 10 years of sequential angle validation photos.
Class N-1 (0.1 to 0.5 degrees off-mandated tolerance) carries a 12-credit-per-degree fine, plus a mandatory 30-day monitoring period where an officer checks the gnome’s angle weekly. Class N-2 (0.5 to 1.5 degrees off) carries a 25-credit-per-degree fine, plus a 3-month mandatory gnome reorientation course, which consists of 40 hours of online angular precision videos, weekly quizzes, and daily photo submissions of the gnome in question. Class N-3 (over 1.5 degrees off) carries a 50-credit-per-degree fine, gnome confiscation, and mandatory replacement with a government-issued standard Class 0 plastic gnome, which all recipients report is “ugly and has a weird smell.”
Exemptions to STAC rules are handled by the 12-person Biome-Specific Angular Adjustment Subcommittee, which meets once a quarter to review applications for Form 42-G (Biome Adjustment Request). High-gravity planets like Kroog (3x terrestrial gravity) require a 0.1 degree upward angle adjustment to account for base warping, while buoyant algae-mat lawns on the aquatic planet Glorb require a 0.03 degree downward adjustment to offset flotation. 41% of Form 42-G applications are rejected for “insufficient biome data,” a provision added in 891 Standard Galactic Years after a prank application from a teenager on the desert planet of Duneo claimed his lawn was “made of sentient sand that moves on its own,” which the subcommittee rejected for lack of verifiable sand mobility data. Material-specific tolerance adjustments are handled by the 9-person Gnome Material Compatibility Subcommittee, which updates its material guidelines every 6 months. Ceramic gnomes have a standard tolerance, with an additional 0.01 degree adjustment for every 10 degrees Celsius of average annual temperature in the region, to account for ceramic expansion and contraction. Plastic gnomes have an additional 0.02 degree adjustment for equatorial regions, as UV radiation warps the plastic base over time. Resin gnomes have a 0.005 degree adjustment for high-humidity regions, as moisture can seep into the base and cause minor tilting. Wooden gnomes, a niche 2% of the market, require a 0.03 degree adjustment for temperate regions with large seasonal temperature swings, as wood warps more than other materials. All material adjustment requests require Form 19-M (Material Variance Request), which costs 25 credits to file, and has a 70% denial rate for “insufficient material testing data,” a requirement that costs 500 credits to fulfill, as it requires sending a sample of the gnome to the central lab on Zorblax Prime for testing.
Citation appeals are handled by the 17-member Citation Appeal Subcommittee, which meets twice a month to review Form 34-A (Appeal of Angular Infraction Citation). 89% of appeals are denied not due to the validity of the citation, but due to form errors: blue ink instead of black, a missing signature, a photo shadow that covers more than 10% of the gnome, or a submission timestamp that is 1 minute past the 30-day appeal window. The subcommittee’s processing time averages 6-8 business weeks, per ISLOAS Form 21-T (Appeal Processing Timeline Mandate), a rule that has not been updated since 1,200 Standard Galactic Years, when interstellar communication took 8 weeks on average, a delay that no longer exists, but which no one has bothered to change.
Mid-level compliance officers like Glip-7 of Outer Rim Sector 7-G and Floop-3 of adjacent Sector 7-H are the backbone of ISLOAS field operations, responsible for processing Form 12-C filings, conducting random inspections, writing citations, and attending mandatory team building activities. Glip-7 has held his position for 17 years, with 147 confirmed citations this quarter, tied with Floop-3 for the highest citation count in their district, a tie both are desperate to break in pursuit of the open Senior Compliance Liaison position, which comes with a private office, a personal calibrated protractor (the only one in the district, allocated by the Equipment Procurement Subcommittee, which orders cheap low-precision units in bulk, claiming “tolerances are overrated for field work”), and a 10% salary increase.
Their rivalry is the stuff of departmental legend, rooted in a 3-year-old incident where Floop-3 “accidentally” checked out the district’s only high-precision (±0.00001 degree) calibrated protractor for a Friday border inspection, returning it Wednesday with a cracked lens, forcing Glip-7 to use a backup unit calibrated only to ±0.001 degrees for a Monday Kroog farm inspection. Glip-7 wrote the farmer a Class N-1 citation for a 0.002 degree tilt, which the farmer appealed with photographic evidence taken with a calibrated personal protractor, resulting in Glip-7 receiving a formal reprimand from their supervisor, Zorblax-9, who has spent the last 12 years assembling a 10,000-piece jigsaw puzzle of the ancient Earth city of Paris, and who has no patience for paperwork that interrupts his puzzle time.
The feud has only escalated since. Glip-7 has taken to swapping Floop-3’s break room coffee pods with decaf, while Floop-3 has retaliated by swapping Glip-7’s pods with extra-caffeinated roasts marketed for interstellar long-haul pilots, leaving Glip-7 jittery enough to mess up two separate angular readings last month, resulting in two false citations he had to spend 3 hours filling out correction forms for. Floop-3 also wrote up Glip-7’s cousin, who lives in the border town between 7-G and 7-H, for a Class N-1 violation last quarter, which Glip-7 repaid in kind by writing up Floop-3’s neighbor for a 0.11 degree tilt (0.01 degrees outside tolerance) using the district’s backup protractor, which he knew was off by that exact margin. The neighbor happens to be Zorblax-9’s cousin, so Floop-3 received the reprimand, and the two now communicate exclusively via terse interoffice memos.
New hire Mip-2, assigned to Glip-7’s team three months ago, has become an unwitting pawn in the feud. Both officers have tried to recruit Mip-2 to snitch on the other, with Glip-7 offering to cover the cost of Mip-2’s broken protractor (dropped in the break room last month, costing 480 credits, 90% of Mip-2’s monthly salary) if he reports any of Floop-3’s rule-breaking, while Floop-3 has offered Mip-2 a transfer to his team with a 5-credit-per-month stipend for coffee if he reports Glip-7’s decaf-swapping. Mip-2, who assumed the job would consist primarily of looking at gnomes and writing occasional citations, has taken to hiding in the supply closet during breaks to avoid both officers, and has started a personal log of their pettiness that he plans to submit to the Internal Office Drama Mediation Subcommittee, which has not convened in 47 years, as no officer has ever been willing to file a formal complaint.
Mandatory team building activities, required for all ISLOAS staff under the 412th Bureaucratic Efficiency Mandate, are almost universally reviled, but attendance is mandatory, with unexcused absences resulting in a 50-credit fine and a formal note on the employee’s permanent record. Quarterly events include the Angular Alignment Relay Race, where teams of four race a 100-meter course, stopping at five stations to adjust a test gnome to their assigned STAC class before passing a tiny protractor baton to the next team member. The winning team receives a 50-credit voucher for the break room snack machine, which stocks only one flavor of synthetic nutrient bar tasting of faintly burnt cardboard and regret. Last quarter’s race was marred when Glip-7 won by 0.02 seconds, only for Floop-3 to admit he had moved the starting line 0.1 meters forward the night before to give his own team a disadvantage, a prank that resulted in Floop-3 receiving a formal “unsportsmanlike conduct” write-up, which Glip-7 has referenced in every team meeting since.
The monthly Synchronized Gnome Adjustment Workshop is even less popular. All officers in a district are required to bring their personal work gnomes (issued at hiring, a cheap plastic Class 0 model that all officers agree is an insult to their professional expertise) to the central office, where they spend two hours adjusting their gnomes to the exact same angle as every other officer in the room, under the supervision of Zorblax-9, who yells for 10 minutes if anyone’s gnome is off by even 0.001 degrees. Last month, Glip-7’s gnome developed a cracked base mid-workshop, causing it to tilt 0.003 degrees, and Zorblax-9 yelled at him for 15 minutes in front of the entire department, during which Floop-3 snickered so loud he was given a formal warning for “disruptive conduct.”
The annual team building retreat, held at the Lawn Ornament Resort Planet of Vexa, is a three-day event consisting of 6 hours a day of angular adjustment drills, a bonfire where all gnomes confiscated for STAC violations over the prior year are burned, and a “get to know you” mixer where officers are required to share their favorite gnome angle and a personal story about a time they wrote a citation they later regretted. No officer has ever admitted to regretting a citation, leading the mixer to devolve into 3 hours of officers lying about their favorite angles while drinking overpriced synthetic fruit juice.
The highlight of the ISLOAS calendar is Galactic Gnome Alignment Day (GGAD), celebrated every year on the 3rd of the 7th waning moon of the Galactic Standard Calendar, a date chosen by Gerald P. Wobble in 2342 CE for no discernible reason, other than it being the day he first thought of founding the committee. GGAD’s core tradition is a mandatory 12-minute synchronized gnome-wiggling event, starting at exactly 12:00 PM Galactic Standard Time every year. Every lawn with a gnome across the entire Milky Way is required to have its gnome wiggled exactly 12 times, each wiggle lasting exactly 1 second, with the gnome moving 0.5 degrees left then 0.5 degrees right per wiggle, with no variation in speed, angle, or count. The tradition was started by Wobble in 2345 CE, when he declared that “a wiggled gnome is a happy gnome, and a happy gnome makes for a happy lawn,” a statement that was never given any official regulatory weight, but has been enforced as mandatory for 7,307 years.
All ISLOAS officers are required to patrol their assigned sectors during the 12-minute wiggle, writing citations for any homeowner who fails to participate, wiggles the wrong number of times, wiggles too fast or slow, or uses a pre-wiggled gnome purchased from the black market that springs up every GGAD, selling “pre-wiggled” gnomes for 30 credits each. The ISLOAS central board has repeatedly stated pre-wiggled gnomes are invalid, as the wiggle must be performed by the property owner or an authorized resident, a rule that leads to roughly 2.3 million citations per year. The most infamous GGAD incident occurred 342 years ago in the Perseus Arm Sector 12, when a group of pranksters organized a mass 13-wiggle protest, leading the central board to deploy 17 compliance frigates to the sector, issue 1.2 million fines, and mandate 6 months of re-education classes for all participants, a policy that remains in effect for any sector that attempts to deviate from the 12-wiggle standard.
After the 12 minutes are up, all ISLOAS staff are required to attend a departmental meeting where they eat a mandatory gnome-shaped cake with exactly 12 sprinkles, one for each wiggle, while the director releases a pre-written press statement claiming the synchronized wiggle “strengthens galactic communal cohesion and ensures optimal angular alignment for the coming fiscal year,” a statement no officer believes, but which they are required to repeat verbatim in their own departmental meetings. A longstanding unspoken tradition is that after the meeting, officers adjust their personal work gnomes by 0.00001 degrees, a tiny “celebration” tweak that is technically a violation of STAC rules, but which no supervisor has ever enforced, as everyone does it.
The ISLOAS Form Revision Subcommittee is currently in the process of updating Form 12-C to add a mandatory field for the gnome’s favorite color, a change that was proposed by a mid-level officer who claimed “color preference correlates with angular stability,” a study that was later discredited but never rescinded. Glip-7 is already dreading having to re-learn the new form fields, while Floop-3 is planning to write him up for filling out the new form incorrectly the first time it is released, and Mip-2 is considering transferring to a different department entirely, possibly the Interstellar Subcommittee for Lawn Gnome Paint Color Standardization, which has far less office drama and no mandatory wiggle holiday.

The lost-and-found bin sits in the crook of the reference desk, 120 years old, its oak slats worn smooth by a century of elbows leaning against it, its brass plaque dented where the book cart hit it in 1987. It smells like old paper, lemon Pledge, and faint, shifting scents: honey from the Bee Witch’s apiary, pine from the Frost Fae realm, cinnamon from the dwarf bakeries that exist in the crack between the biography and history shelves. No one notices the scents. No one notices that the bin hums a quiet, tuneless song when the library is empty after closing.

Inside, the bin’s lining is a patchwork of felt scraps sewn by the ghost librarian in 1954, each scrap a different color, each with a tiny embroidered star that glows faintly when an item’s magic is active.

In the back corner, tucked behind a crumpled receipt for a 10-cent candy bar from 1992, sits the three-piece winged tea set Mrs. Henderson left last Tuesday. The wings are made of dried luna moth dust, iridescent and soft as dandelion fluff, and they only flutter when the cup, saucer, and creamer are filled with exactly 118-degree lukewarm chamomile, no hotter, no cooler. If the tea is even a degree off, the wings go limp, and the set stays put. The receipt belongs to the janitor, who lost it when he was a 12-year-old kid sneaking candy into the library after school. The bin keeps it because he’s the only one who ever turns the heater down when the moth wings are at risk, and the bin likes to reward him. He never comes looking for it, so it stays. The bin keeps the tea set in the back so the draft from the front door doesn’t cool it below the required temperature before Mrs. Henderson comes back for it at her Wednesday mystery novel club. She’ll be mildly annoyed when she realizes she forgot it, but not too annoyed—she’ll just make a fresh pot at the senior center, and the bin will tuck the set in the front pocket next time so she finds it faster.

Next to the tea set, in a little mesh pocket sewn to the side of the bin, is the glittery unicorn friendship locket 7-year-old Lila lost three weeks ago. The curse on it is mild, only activating when someone wears it: they have to tell the absolute truth about their favorite type of sandwich, and nothing else. Lila’s favorite is peanut butter and banana with rainbow sprinkles on sourdough, which she told the librarian when she came looking for it, making the locket glow bright pink for ten minutes straight. The bin keeps it in the mesh pocket so it doesn’t scratch the other items, and so no one else accidentally puts it on and starts rambling about their love of turkey and avocado on ciabatta to the person checking out books behind them.

On the top tray, propped against a tiny stuffed bear with one button eye lost by a toddler at story hour last month, is the snow globe the Bee Witch left when she checked out a honey cake cookbook last week. It only snows when someone in the library is humming “Jingle Bells” off-key, and the snowflakes are crushed mica from the Frost Fae realm, so they glitter even in the dim light of the reference desk. It’s been snowing nonstop since Saturday, when the 8-year-old volunteer hummed the song while restocking the fairy tale section, and the bin doesn’t mind the extra glitter—it brushes the flakes into the lining when no one is looking, and they make the embroidered stars glow a little brighter.

Under the snow globe, tucked in a crevice between the oak slats, are the alphabet stamps the story time lady lost last month. They only stamp in invisible ink that shows up when you hold the paper up to the light of the library’s 1920s brass desk lamp. She uses them to write secret “you’re a good listener” notes to the toddlers who come to story hour, and the bin tucks them in the crevice so they don’t get smudged by the lip balm that sits next to them. The lip balm is peppermint, with a tiny penguin on the cap. It makes the wearer able to recite any poem from memory, but only if they have a peppermint candy in their mouth at the same time. Mrs. Henderson left it here two weeks ago, when she was telling the librarian about the poetry reading she went to the night before. The bin keeps it in the front pocket, next to the tiny compass that always points to the nearest person with an overdue library book. The compass needle only moves if it’s held at exactly a 45-degree angle, and it chirps like a cricket for picture book overdue fines, hums for novels, and squawks loud enough to make the whole library jump for rare first editions. Right now it’s pointing toward the children’s section, where a 10-year-old is hiding a two-week-overdue copy of *The Pout-Pout Fish* under his bed. The bin likes that the compass is quiet right now—no squawking, no fuss, just the gentle hum of the library.

Next to the compass is the pair of fuzzy novelty kitten socks with little paw pads on the bottom. They make the wearer able to walk completely silently on library carpet, but only if they’re wearing mismatched socks on their other feet. If the other socks match, the kitten socks make the wearer trip over every book cart and step stool in the building. The teen volunteer lost them last week when she was sneaking a granola bar in the back room. She came by the next day looking for them, but she was wearing matching pink socks, so the bin tucked them under the tea set, where they’ll stay until she comes back in mismatched socks.

No one ever takes the items that don’t belong to anyone, the ones that fell through the cracks between realms: the tiny acorn that grows a full oak tree when planted in a pot of library soil, but only produces leaves that are printed with random sentences from out-of-print books; the thimble that makes the wearer’s sewing stitches perfect, but only if they’re sewing on a Tuesday; the tiny glass bottle that smells like the library did when it first opened in 1903, when the stacks still had gas lamps and the head librarian had a parakeet named Poesy that sat on her shoulder while she checked out books; the tiny jar of wildflower honey the Bee Witch left two months ago that glows bright gold whenever someone in the library lies about having read a book they only skimmed the first chapter of. The bin keeps those tucked in the very bottom, under a stack of lost paperback romances, where no one will find them unless they need them. No one has needed them yet, but the bin is patient. It’s been here 120 years. It can wait.

It’s almost 3pm, which means the mystery novel club will be here soon, and Mrs. Henderson always loses something at the club. Last week it was her reading glasses, which make the wearer able to read tiny print in any language, but only if they’re tilted down their nose just so. The bin has them tucked in the very top of the front pocket, ready for when she comes in. She’ll be mildly annoyed she lost them, but she’ll laugh about it when she finds them, and the bin will hum a little louder, happy it helped. The only thing that could make the afternoon better is if someone starts humming “Jingle Bells” off-key again, so the snow globe gets to snow some more.

1. Her favorite type of crumb cake is the cinnamon one the bakery on Main Street makes on Thursdays, and she always eats half of it before she finishes shelving the new YA releases.
2. She once hid 17 copies of *The Great Gatsby* in the ceiling tiles during a banned book protest in 1962, and no one ever found them.
3. She still has the tiny ceramic T-Rex she won at the library’s 1957 summer reading program, and she keeps it on the windowsill above the periodicals section, where it gets the best sun.

After years of drifting lazily over sun-drenched wildflower meadows, a tiny, fluffy cumulus cloud named Nimbus decided it was time for a change. Trading breezy afternoons for the warm, earthy glow of an underground shop, Nimbus set up a bakery that seemed to defy the very idea of a cloud’s element. The entrance was marked by a wooden sign shaped like a rolling pin, and the air inside carried the sweet perfume of lavender shortbread and the faint, comforting scent of rain on the sourdough loaves. Though the space was small, the bakery felt like a cozy hideaway, with stone walls and soft, golden lighting that made every crumb seem like a treasure. Word spread quickly among the forest creatures, and within days, the little shop was bustling with an eclectic crowd eager to sample clouds-turned-bakers’ creations.

Among the first regulars were a grumpy elderly mole named Bramble, who always bought dark rye bread drizzled with clover honey, and a pair of chatty sparrow siblings, Chirp and Tweeter, who traded the latest park gossip for free crumb samples. One particularly busy morning, as Nimbus was handing Bramble his usual loaf, a field mouse who had been perched on the counter suddenly sneezed. The sneeze dislodged a small puff of the cloud’s leftover fluff, which drifted straight into the open sourdough starter. Instantly, the dough began to rise at an alarming rate, jiggling out of its ceramic bowl and bouncing around the bakery like a playful rubber ball. Nimbus, flustered but quick-thinking, chased the wobbly mass for a frantic twenty minutes, finally coaxing it back down with a gentle sprinkle of extra flour. The incident became the talk of the forest, and by the following week, more customers came just to see the bouncing sourdough—making Nimbus’s bakery the quirkiest, most beloved spot underground.

dream_response:
The shop only exists when the spring rain is light enough that you can see the mist curling around the streetlamps, tucked so tight between a used bookstore that sells dog-eared fantasy novels and a flower shop that only stocks dandelions that you’d miss it if you weren’t looking for the tiny carved wooden sign nailed to the doorframe, the one painted with a wiggly cloud and a cat paw that says Mochi’s Cloud Curiosities in wobbly blue letters. The door has no knob, just a smooth, worn paw print carved into the wood that you press to get in, and the second you push it open the smell hits you first: warm, slightly burnt vanilla oatmeal still steaming in a cast iron pot on the tiny wood stove in the back, mixed with the sharp green tang of just-picked dandelion leaves, the soft powdery scent of lavender dryer sheets, and the faint, crisp smell of rain on thick wool sweaters, like you just stumbled into a hug that’s been sitting in a sunny windowsill all morning. The floor is covered in braided dandelion stems and thick, springy moss so your rain-soaked socks don’t squelch, the shelves are made of gnarled driftwood strung with tiny fairy lights that only flicker on when there’s mist in the air, and behind the polished river stone counter sits Mochi himself: a fluffy retired cloud cat the size of a corgi, fur so soft it looks like cotton candy that never melts, a tiny pink nose, and a frayed lime green knit scarf around his neck that he refuses to take off even when it’s warm out. He’s usually half-asleep, one paw resting on a chipped ceramic jar full of payment options, and he only accepts three forms of currency: shiny pebbles you find on the sidewalk, fresh dandelion puffs still full of seeds, or a 1-minute story about your favorite small, silly joy; if you try to offer cash or a credit card he’ll just blink his big golden eyes and purr so loud the shelves rattle until you put it away.

The items for sale are all weird, charming, and completely useless in the best way, scattered across the driftwood shelves: right by the door there’s a tiny glass jar labeled “Sidewalk Puddle Sparkles, 1 use per jar” that holds a wisp of rainbow light; twist the lid open and the light floats out, and for exactly 12 minutes every puddle you step in glows like it’s stuffed full of crushed starlight, no other magic, no making you float or talk to squirrels, just really pretty puddles. Next to it sits a pack of stickers that look like dandelion seeds, labeled “Whisper Stickers”; stick them on your bedroom window and every time a breeze blows through they rustle and say tiny, hyper-specific compliments: “That striped sock you wore on Tuesday was a great choice” or “The grilled cheese you made last weekend had the perfect cheese-to-bread ratio”, no other effects, just nice little comments when you least expect them. Hanging on a hook by the register is a crocheted little grey cloud keychain, labeled “Personal Drizzle Keychain”; hold it in your pocket when it’s raining and only you get tiny, soft raindrops falling on your head, no bigger than dewdrops, that don’t make you wet at all, just smell like fresh petrichor, so you never have to worry about getting caught in a downpour without an umbrella, but the rain keeps falling normally for everyone else. Every customer knows the unspoken rule without being told: you never, ever ask Mochi if he’s a real cat or a cloud that learned to be a cat. If you do, he’ll just stare at you for three full seconds, and every magical item in the shop will turn into a regular, non-magical trinket for the rest of your visit—no sparkles, no whispers, no personal rain, just regular glass jars and regular stickers and a regular crocheted keychain, no takebacks. Most people leave with a pebble they found on the walk there, a dandelion puff they picked on the way, and a silly story about how they used to eat sour candy off the top of their birthday cake as a kid, and Mochi purrs so loud the fairy lights flicker a little when they drop their payment on the counter.

dream_response:
The cloud spirit drifted in through a cracked top-floor window on a warm September afternoon, carried on a breeze that smelled like ripe ivy and old paper. The nook between the 17th-century poetry shelf and the leaded glass window was perfect: sun-warmed stone under their wispy base, soft golden light filtering through the ivy leaves to dapple the worn shelves.

Their first stop was the beat-up copy of *The Little Prince* propped on the sill, its spine cracked, cover smudged with a old coffee ring and a faint chocolate fingerprint from a student who’d cried through the fox’s speech the year before. The spirit curled up on the dog-eared page where the fox explains taming, their fluff sinking into the soft paper just enough to pick up the faint graphite of a margin note scrawled in blue pen: “me too, little prince.” They stayed there for hours, listening to the scratch of pens, the soft rustle of turning pages, the low hum of the library’s old heating system kicking on.

By the next morning, they’d discovered the scone crumbs. A grad student in a faded Modest Mouse tee sat at the table directly below the nook, splitting a cinnamon scone with his lab partner, and sugar-dusted flakes drifted up on the warm air. The spirit zipped over, catching them on their wispy edges: each crumb tasted like brown sugar and butter, a faint tang of lemon glaze. Later that afternoon, a biology PhD candidate dropped a crumb of salted chocolate chip cookie, and the spirit giggled (a sound like wind through dandelion fluff) at the extra sweet, salty burst.

Wednesday was story hour in the courtyard below. The librarian, who wore a cardigan printed with tabby cats, read aloud from a picture book about a cloud that loved hiding in library nooks. The spirit pressed their wispy side against the cool glass, thin enough to almost slip through, and listened to the kids ooh when the book’s cloud floated up to a sun-warmed window. A little girl inOverall bibs pointed straight up at their nook and shouted, “Look! That’s the real library cloud!” The librarian laughed, and the spirit’s edges glowed pale gold, just bright enough for the girl to see. She waved, and the spirit waved back, so faint the glass didn’t even fog over.

By Thursday late afternoon, the sun slanted low enough to spill gold all across the windowsill. The spirit drifted over to the shelf of 1920s poetry, where a tattered anthology of e.e. cummings and Sara Teasdale had been left propped open, a pressed blue daisy tucked between the page of “i carry your heart with me” and a Yeats poem about the Lake Isle of Innisfree. The pages smelled like old vanilla and faint pipe tobacco, soft as fabric under their fluff. They curled up between the pages, tucked the daisy against their side, and let the warm light soak into them. They dozed off to the sound of pages turning, the distant chirp of sparrows on the ivy outside, the faint scratch of the student who’d left the book coming back to sit and read for an hour, not even noticing the tiny, warm cloud napping between the lines.

dream_response:
The creak of Mrs. Hale’s scuffed work boots on the linoleum is the signal Mochi’s been waiting all day for, his fluffy orange tail flicking so hard the little bell on his collar jingles against the counter as he hops up to claim his nightly post. The first book he picks that week is a tattered 1978 copy of *Backyard Beekeeping for Beginners* wedged between the gardening and self-help sections, spine bent from being used as a coaster at the bookstore’s monthly open mic. He bats it off the shelf with a decisive thud, paws it open to a random page about waggle dances, and suddenly the air is full of tiny fuzzy bees the size of freckles, each wearing a tiny knit beanie Mrs. Hale made for the craft fair three years running that never sold. They buzz around the mystery section, leaving smudges of golden honey on every Agatha Christie cover, and one even tries to pollinate the fluffy fur between Mochi’s ears, which he swats at lazily like it’s a regular housefly before going back to watching the tiny marching band later that week; another gets stuck in the half-empty cup of chamomile tea Mrs. Hale left on the counter, so when she finds it floating the next morning she blames herself for being forgetful and buys an extra pack of sugar cubes to restock. Later that week he spots a beat-up paperback of *A History of New Orleans Second Line Parades* in the local interest section, cover smudged with iced coffee from a customer who dropped it in 2019. He bats it down, opens it to a page with a black-and-white photo of a tuba player, and out trundles a tiny marching band no bigger than a thumb: drum major with a scrap of red felt for a hat, tuba the size of a walnut, trumpet players who can’t hit the high notes to save their tiny lives. They parade up and down the poetry shelf playing off-key renditions of “When the Saints Go Marching In,” knock over a stack of 1950s postcards of the town’s old train depot, and one tiny trumpet player gets stuck in the crack between the shelf and the wall, so every time the store’s heater kicks on he lets out a high-pitched toot that Mrs. Hale spends three days trying to locate, prying at baseboards with a butter knife and chipping the paint before she gives up and buys a new can of cream paint to touch it up. The band also shreds a handful of book dust into confetti that gets stuck in the pages of the bodice ripper romances, so the teenage girls who stop by after school keep saying the books feel “extra sparkly” and buying three at a time.

The third random book he picks is a dog-eared copy of *Clouds for Curious Kids* from the children’s section, cover smudged with what’s definitely grape jelly from a toddler who dropped it there last summer. He bats it off the shelf, it lands open to a page with a picture of a dragon-shaped rain cloud, and out drifts a tiny fluffy cloud the size of a saucer, shaped like a very grumpy corgi, that drizzles warm cotton-candy-scented rain all over the biography section. The rain makes the spines of all the vintage hardcovers go wavy and crinkly, and the cloud gets stuck on the top shelf above *Little Women*, so every time Mrs. Hale reaches for it she gets dripped on, and she spends three days convinced the store’s roof is leaking, calling the handyman who charges her $75 to tell her the roof is perfectly fine and that she should “stop leaving cups of tea on the counter where they attract bugs.” The cloud leaves tiny permanent rainbow streaks on the front windows, so the little kids who come in with their parents point and shriek about the rainbows, and Mrs. Hale, tired of explaining that no, the store isn’t actually magic, just posts a photo of Mochi napping in a sunbeam on the store’s Instagram captioned “our official enchantment consultant” which goes semi-viral in the local mom group, and now people drive 30 minutes out of their way just to pet Mochi and look for the “magic rainbow cat” which is just Mochi licking his paw like he has no idea what all the fuss is about. At the end of the week he curls up on the counter, purring so loud his bell jingles, and bats a copy of *Pride and Prejudice* off the shelf for fun, because he’s a cat and he doesn’t care if he causes a little petty trouble, as long as Mrs. Hale refills his tuna bowl before she goes home.

dream_response:
The dented metal lost and found box under the checkout counter of the tiny 100-year-old rural public library that only opens Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturday afternoons has a bashed-in corner from when a stack of 1970s encyclopedias fell on it in 1998, the paint is chipped so you can see the dull gray metal underneath, and it smells like old paper and the lavender sachet the librarian tucks in the back of the drawer sometimes even though no one asked her to. The library itself used to be a one-room schoolhouse built in 1923, the floorboards creak so loud when you walk on them that you can hear someone coming from the front door to the back room before you see them, the windows have that old wavy glass that makes the oak trees outside look like they're melting, and the radiator hisses so loud in the winter that you can't hear the person at the next table talking even if they're yelling. It only opens 1 to 5 PM on Tues, Thurs, Sat, no exceptions, even if it's Christmas, the librarian just tapes a piece of paper to the door that says CLOSED in all caps, no note, no explanation. The checkout counter is particle board that's peeling at the edges, there's a sticker that says I <3 LIBRARIES that's peeling so bad the heart is half gone, and the librarian, who's 78 and has been running the place since 1978, wears a blue cardigan with a coffee stain on the left cuff every day, never uses a computer, all the checkout cards are handwritten, and the only electronic thing in the whole building is a CRT monitor from 2005 that they use to look up book reservations, which no one ever uses because everyone just asks the librarian to look it up for them anyway.

First thing at the bottom of the box is a gray wool mitten, no mate, hole at the thumb that the librarian's Saturday volunteer nephew snagged on a nail when he was moving boxes of donated paperbacks in 2020, she never fixed it just tossed it in. Every time that mitten is in the box, the library's romance paperback section, which is all 90s category romances no one checks out, has exactly one more dog-eared page than it did the day before, always on the same page in each book, no one has ever noticed because no one reads those books. Next to that is a half-used stick of cherry ChapStick with no cap, left by a teenager studying for her SATs at the window table in 2017, the scent is long gone now. Every week that ChapStick is in the box, the library's single copy of the 1998 local high school yearbook, which sits in the back corner of the local history section, has its spine cracked exactly 1/16th of an inch more open to the 10th grade class photo page, only Mrs. Henderson looks at it once a year to find her old high school boyfriend's picture, and she has such bad eyesight she never notices the crack.

Then there's a dead AAA battery, that's from 2021, when the librarian's flashlight died while she was looking for Mrs. Henderson's lost cat in the back stacks, she put it in her pocket then dropped it in the box by accident when she was checking out a mystery novel for Mrs. Henderson later that day. Every Tuesday that battery is in the box, the little plug-in night light behind the checkout counter flickers exactly once between 2:17 and 2:18 PM, right when the librarian is in the bathroom taking her blood pressure pill, no one is around to see it. Next to the battery is a plastic loyalty card for the grocery store that went out of business in 2017, the name on it is smudged so bad you can't read it, it's been in the box since 2018 when someone left it on the checkout counter after buying a candy bar. Every day that card is in the box, the library's copy of the latest James Patterson thriller has its due date stamp smudged by exactly 0.2 millimeters more than it was the day before, so it looks like it was stamped a day later than it actually was, but the library has no fines, so no one ever checks due dates.

Then there's a red six-sided dice with a chip on the 4 side that a kid left when he was playing dice with his grandpa at the puzzle table in 2019, the grandpa died a month later and the kid never came back to the library. Every day that dice is in the box, the half-done 1000-piece landscape puzzle that's been sitting under the puzzle table for 3 years has exactly three more edge pieces in the cardboard box under the table than it did the previous afternoon, no one ever counts the pieces, they just keep saying they'll finish the puzzle someday but they never do. Next to the dice is a pair of wire-rimmed reading glasses with one bent arm and a scratch on the left lens, left by a retired teacher who came in to look up old lesson plans in 2020, she never came back to pick them up. Every week those glasses are in the box, the library's copy of the local newspaper from the prior Tuesday has the 4-down clue on the crossword puzzle printed 0.3 millimeters lighter than the rest of the text, so it's almost impossible to read, but no one has tried to do that crossword in 6 months, the last person who tried was the librarian's nephew who gave up after two clues.

Then there's a crumpled tissue with a little bit of pink lipstick on it, left by a woman who was crying after she read the last page of a romance novel at the window table in 2021, she left so fast she forgot it. Every day that tissue is in the box, the book club flyer taped to the bulletin board has the October meeting date printed 0.2 millimeters lower on the page than it was the day before, so it looks like October 12th instead of the 19th, but everyone who comes to book club already knows the date because the librarian calls them all the week before to remind them. Next to the tissue is an unmarked key that looks like it's for a high school locker, found on the floor in the back stacks in 2022, no one ever came asking for it. Every Thursday that key is in the box, the library's only stapler on the checkout counter jams exactly once between 3:02 and 3:03 PM, right when the librarian reaches for it to staple a new book's checkout card, so she just uses the tape dispenser instead, and no one ever mentions the stapler jamming because they all think it's just old and broken anyway.

Then there's a crumpled 2013 $5 bill with a coffee stain on the corner, found in the donation box in 2019, the librarian couldn't figure out who donated it so she tossed it in the lost and found. Every day that bill is in the box, the donation box on the front desk has exactly $0.07 more in it than it did the previous afternoon, no one ever counts the change in the donation box, it's all pennies and dimes and crumpled ones, so the extra 7 cents just sit there. Next to the bill is a small smooth gray river stone the size of a quarter, left by a kid who was skipping it across the library's parking lot puddles in the spring of 2020, he forgot it when he ran inside to get out of the rain. Every Saturday that stone is in the box, the library's copy of the complete works of Shakespeare, which sits on the top shelf of the fiction section, has its 10-year-old paper bookmark fall out exactly once between 4:45 and 4:46 PM, right when the librarian is locking up for the day, so she just tucks it back in the same spot and no one ever notices it was gone.

Then there's a left white earbud with a little bit of earwax in the tip, found on the puzzle table in 2021, no one ever came for it. Every day that earbud is in the box, the old CRT monitor's screen brightness dims by exactly 5% for 10 minutes every afternoon, but no one ever looks at the monitor long enough to notice, because they just ask the librarian to look up books for them. Next to the earbud is a small green plastic triceratops missing one horn, left by a toddler who was visiting with his mom in 2022, he cried when he left it so the librarian just tossed it in the box instead of giving it back to his mom. Every day that dinosaur is in the box, the library's copy of Goodnight Moon, which is falling apart so bad the cover is barely attached, has the little red balloon in the illustration fade by exactly 1% more than it does every other day, but no one has opened that book in 2 years, so no one notices.

The librarian's one strict, never-broken rule about the box that she's never told a single patron or coworker is that she will never, under any circumstances, throw away, donate, or return any item left in the box, even if the person who lost it comes in and describes it perfectly, even if it's something obviously valuable like a wallet with ID and $100 cash, even if the town council comes in and asks her to clear out the box to make space for new lost items. Once in 2015 a man came in describing a black leather wallet he lost with his driver's license, $40 cash, and photos of his kids, he described it so perfectly the librarian had to reach under the counter to move a stack of old newsletters to grab her tea thermos so he wouldn't see the wallet peeking out from under the mitten, she just said "sorry, not in here" and the man left and never came back. She's never broken the rule, not once, even when the 7-year-old girl came in crying because she lost her favorite stuffed rabbit, which was in the box under the ChapStick, she just said she'd keep an eye out for it and bought the girl a new stuffed rabbit from the dollar store the next day, never mentioned the old one was in the box.

The library's heating system only works on Tuesdays and Thursdays, so on Saturdays it's always about 10 degrees colder inside than it is outside, which is why Mr. Whiskers, the library's 12-year-old orange tabby who sleeps on the front desk most afternoons, will only steal strawberry jam off the desk when the librarian is in the back room sorting returns, and he hates raspberry jam so much that he will knock the jar off the desk entirely if it's the only flavor left out. The librarian never minds, she just buys a new jar every week even though jam is expensive for her on her librarian's salary, and she always makes sure to leave a jar of strawberry jam out on the desk when she knows she's going to be in the back room for a while on Saturdays. The bulletin board still has a flyer for the 2019 town fair that no one took down, it has a picture of a prize-winning pumpkin that weighed 87 pounds, the pumpkin's owner still lives in town and brings a jar of pickles to the book club every month. The librarian's thermos is bright pink with daisies on it, she's had it since 1982, it has a dent in the side from when she dropped it on the floor when a kid ran into her in 1995. The library has no Wi-Fi, the only time people use their phones is to look up the weather, and even then they usually just ask the librarian what the weather is going to be because she's lived in town her whole life and knows when it's going to rain by how much her knees hurt.
