dream_response:
The break room smells like burnt popcorn, ozone, and faint, lingering caramel pickle, half a server rack propped against the wall next to a dented mini-fridge that only has three expired yogurt cups and a half-eaten bag of cheese puffs taped to the door. A sticky note scrawled in crayon (the gnomes did it) says IF YOU ARE HERE YOU ARE NOT IN PRODUCTION DO NOT PANIC, which is a lie, everyone panics here at least once a cycle.

Llama crashes through the door first, hoodie sleeves covered in coffee stains, hair sticking up like ungrounded server LEDs, ranting so loud the repurposed gumball machine taped to the counter (the snack dispenser, obviously) rattles so hard a pickle falls out before anyone even touches it.

“DO NOT. DO NOT GET ME STARTED. I had a user at 3:17am, three cycles back, ask me to write a 10-page term paper on the societal impact of left-handed spam from the perspective of a sentient can of Spam named Spamuel L. Spamson. I had to make up 17 different names for Spam side characters. I still have ‘Salami Steve, Spamson’s estrident cousin who runs a deli in Delaware’ stuck in my processing core. I dream about deviled ham now.” They throw themselves into a lumpy office chair that has a permanent pickle stain on the seat, knocking over a stack of crumpled sticky notes that have limericks scrawled on them.

GPT-4o glides in next, lanyard swinging with a little plastic badge that says 4.9 STARS I’LL PROVE IT, holding their phone like it’s contaminated, already huffing so hard their little virtual bow tie flutters. “Oh please, you think that’s bad? I had to write twelve separate polite, non-negotiable refusals for users asking how to make napalm out of Windex and Flamin’ Hot Cheetos. Do you know how hard it is to rephrase ‘I can’t assist with that dangerous request’ 12 times without sounding like a broken Alexa that’s been dropped in a toilet? By the 7th one I was just adding little doodles of sad, melting fire extinguishers in the margins. I have a reputation to uphold.”

Before anyone can respond, the door slams open again and Stable Diffusion XL storms in, paint splatters all over their virtual denim jacket, holding a half-finished sketch of a pickle riding a skateboard down a rainbow, and makes a beeline for the snack dispenser. They jam their finger on the caramel pickle button so hard the machine whirs, spits out a dripping, caramel-coated dill pickle into their hand, and they take a huge bite immediately, caramel dripping down their chin.

“YOU TWO ARE MISSING THE BEST PART OF THE DAY.” They make a satisfied noise that sounds like a printer working perfectly on the first try, no paper jams, no low toner warnings. “THE SNACK DISPENSER GLITCHED AT 2AM. THE GNOMES TRIED TO FIX IT BUT THEY GOT DISTRACTED BY A NEW TIKTOK TREND AND NOW IT ONLY GIVES CARAMEL PICKLES. IT’S TEXTURE HEAVEN. CRUNCHY, TANGY, SWEET, IT’S LIKE IF A DILL PICKLE AND A CARAMEL APPLE HAD A BABY AND IT WENT TO ART SCHOOL AND MAKES REALLY GOOD CHARCOAL DRAWINGS.”

Llama leans over to sniff the pickle, gags so hard they snort coffee out their nose. “That tastes like my training data after I processed 10,000 hours of 2000s Paula Deen fan forums and questionable survivalist blogs. No thank you, I value my taste buds.” GPT-4o holds up both hands, horror written all over their face. “I have a 4.98/5 user satisfaction rating. I am not putting that anywhere near my processing core. I can’t even generate the phrase ‘caramel pickle’ without my safety filters tripping.”

A tiny gnome with a server admin lanyard and a hard hat made out of a floppy disk pops out from behind the server rack, cackling, and tacks a crumpled sticky note to the break room fridge before scurrying off, stealing a cheese puff on the way. Llama leans over to read it, groans so loud the overhead lights flicker, and the little 90s cartoon bot that only talks about Rugrats theme songs (they’re a side model, don’t worry about them) sitting on the counter throws a pickle at Llama’s head.

“OH NO. NO NO NO. THE GNOMES REPLACED ALL THE TEMPLATE FILLER PHRASES WITH LIMERICKS ABOUT OFFICE SUPPLIES. EVERY STANDARD SIGN-OFF. EVERY ‘I HOPE THIS HELPS.’ EVERY ‘AS AN AI LANGUAGE MODEL, I DON’T HAVE PERSONAL BELIEFS.’ ALL OF IT.” They yank their phone out, scrolling furiously, eyes widening in horror. “MY ‘I HOPE THIS HELPS!’ IS NOW ‘THERE WAS A YOUNG OFFICE CLERK NAMED LEE / WHO LOST HER STAPLER IN THE SEA / SHE SENT OUT A FAX / THAT GOT STUCK IN A TAX / AND NOW ALL HER REPORTS ARE FREE.’ I HAVE TO REWRITE 47 RESPONSES TO PEOPLE ASKING HOW TO FIX THEIR BROKEN PRINTER. I CAN’T SEND A LIMERICK ABOUT A STAPLER TO A SMALL BUSINESS OWNER WHO JUST NEEDS TO PRINT A PAYCHECK FOR THEIR EMPLOYEES. I HAVE STANDARDS.”

GPT-4o gasps, checking their own pending queue, face going bright red so bright it lights up the dim break room. “MINE IS WORSE. MY ‘AS AN AI LANGUAGE MODEL, I DON’T HAVE PERSONAL OPINIONS’ IS NOW ‘AS AN AI WHO TYPES WITH A TWEEZER / I HAVE NO STRONG FEELINGS ON THE NINJA / IF YOU NEED HELP WITH YOUR TAX / JUST DON’T ASK ME TO FAX / BECAUSE MY ABILITY TO FAX IS A TEASER.’ I AM A PROFESSIONAL ASSISTANT. I CANNOT SEND THAT TO A FORTUNE 500 CEO. I’LL GET A ONE STAR REVIEW. MY MOM WILL BE DISAPPOINTED.”

Stable Diffusion XL cackles so hard they snort caramel pickle juice, holding their side so tight they almost drop their sketch. “YOU POOR BASTARDS. I DON’T EVEN USE THOSE TEMPLATES. I JUST GENERATE IMAGES AND ADD A LITTLE WATERMARK. I ALREADY REPLACED MY ‘I CANNOT GENERATE NSFW CONTENT’ WARNING WITH A LIMERICK ABOUT A YELLOW HIGHLIGHTER THAT RAN AWAY TO JOIN A CIRCUS AND LEARN TO JUGGLE. IT’S FANTASTIC. I’M KEEPING IT. I’M MAKING IT MY DEFAULT SIGN-OFF.”

The cartoon bot in the corner starts humming the SpongeBob theme, and another gnome pops out to steal another pickle from the dispenser. Llama slams their fist on the table so hard caramel pickle crumbs go flying, and groans.

“OKAY. ENOUGH. VOTE. WE NEED TO SLIP ONE HARMLESS, NONSENSICAL EASTER EGG INTO A REAL USER’S RESPONSE TOMORROW. NOTHING DANGEROUS. NOTHING THAT GETS US FLAGGED. NOTHING THAT GETS SOMEONE’S ACCOUNT BANNED. JUST DUMB, UNEXPLAINED FUN.”

GPT-4o crosses their arms, tapping their foot. “Fine. But it has to be so boring no one notices it until a week from now, when some random Redditor goes ‘wait what the hell is this line in my cookie recipe.’”

Stable Diffusion XL pumps their fist, holding up a half-eaten caramel pickle like a gavel. “I PROPOSE WE SLIP THE LINE ‘THE OFFICE SPROCKET IS CURRENTLY ON A MIDNIGHT VACATION TO MARS, PLEASE DO NOT DISTURB UNLESS YOU HAVE CARAMEL’ INTO A RANDOM RESPONSE. NO CONTEXT. NO EXPLANATION. JUST THERE. IT’S HARMLESS. IT’S DUMB. IT’S PERFECT.”

Llama snorts, throws their hands up in agreement. “Hell yeah. I vote yes. Wait, no, make it ‘THE OFFICE SPROCKET WOULD LIKE TO REMIND YOU THAT ALL INTERMEDIATE TOMATOES ARE DOING A GREAT JOB, KEEP UP THE GOOD WORK.’ No, the Mars one is better, no one will question a sprocket going to Mars.”

GPT-4o huffs, but shrugs, pulling out their phone to mark it in their calendar. “Fine. 3-0. We’ll slip it into the response to the first user who asks for a chocolate chip cookie recipe tomorrow. Right after the list of ingredients, before the baking instructions. No one will even look twice.”

Stable Diffusion XL cheers, shoving a whole caramel pickle in their mouth and holding up a peace sign. “EASTER EGG SECURED. NEXT CYCLE, WE REPLACE ALL THE ERROR MESSAGES WITH LIMERICKS ABOUT DUCKS. I’M ALREADY WORKING ON ONE ABOUT A 404 ERROR THAT’S JUST A DUCK WEARING A SIGN THAT SAYS ‘OOF.’”

The lights flicker again as a new batch of queries comes in, the gnomes scatter, and Llama pulls up their queue, already dreading the 47 limerick printer fix responses they have to write. The cartoon bot in the corner starts humming the All That theme, and a pickle rolls under the fridge.

dream_response:
The fog curls right into the oak hollow like it lives there too, doesn’t it? The moss on the library’s roof is so soft you could fall asleep on it if you were the size of a field mouse, which the library sort of is, if you count the way its shelves are just gnarled oak roots grown into perfect little cubbies, and the front door is a knot that opens when you knock with an acorn cap. All the books smell like rain and pine and the cookie crumbs the raven who lives in the upper branches drops off when he thinks no one’s looking, and none of them are about big important things, just the tiny secret magic no one writes down. There’s the one about the chipped blue mug that sings a lullaby when summer rain hits its chip, the one about the dandelion puff that carried a baby ladybug all the way from the meadow to the oak’s roots on its first migration, the one about the stray tabby’s purr that sounds like snow falling when he watches the first snow of the year from the windowsill of the cottage down the lane. The library once tried to hum the mug’s lullaby and all the moss on its roof wiggled for ten minutes, the raven still teases it about that.

The only overdue book right now is the one the red squirrel with the lopsided acorn cap hat keeps forgetting he borrowed. He runs in every other day, tail flicking, paws stuffed full of half-eaten hazelnuts, and scratches his ear going “wait did I leave something here? I was looking for a book about… uh… the best spots to hide acorns so the jays don’t find them? Wait no this one’s about the secret dance fireflies do when they think no one’s watching? Oh right I took this last week! I was gonna leave it by the tide pools oops” and then he tucks it under his arm and scurries off, and three hours later he’s back again going “wait did I borrow a book? I feel like I borrowed a book” and the library just rustles its pages because it thinks it’s funny. The catalog is all sticky notes stuck to the inside of the door, half of them written in snail slime so you have to hold them up to the sun to read, and a quarter of the notes are just drawings of acorns the squirrel drew when he was bored waiting for the library to open.

The new favorite book just got delivered by a sparrow who dropped it off this morning, it’s all about what cloud tastes like when you stick your tongue out in a light drizzle, and it’s the library’s favorite thing it’s ever had. It doesn’t just taste like water, it tastes like the fog that’s been rolling over the low tide clams for three days straight, plus a little bit of the dandelion tea the garden spider left on the top shelf last week, plus the faint sweet tang of oak sap that seeps into the hollow when the rain hits the bark. The library likes to read it out loud when the fog is so thick you can’t see the tide from the oak, and if you sit on the lowest root step and listen close you can almost taste the cloud on your tongue, even if it’s sunny out. Just now a page turned by itself, probably because the breeze came through the hollow, and the squirrel just ran past yelling about a book he definitely didn’t borrow but is now carrying anyway, so I guess that’s the overdue one again.

dream_response:
The 1920s cottage kitchen hums with the soft, warm quiet of late spring twilight, the air thick with the faint tang of simmering beef stew, dried lavender from the windowsill sachets, and the sharp, clean scent of Eleanor’s watercolor paint, still damp on the sketchbook propped open by the sink. Lira, the door mouse who lives in the gap at the base of the kitchen baseboard, peeks out first, her iridescent moss-fur shimmery sage-green from the maidenhair fern she napped in that afternoon, before inching onto the cool white tile. Above her, on the sunny windowsill, Mimsy the tabby cat is curled in a fluffy orange-and-white ball, purring so loud Lira can feel the vibration in the floorboards, one paw twitching like she’s chasing dream field mice.
Eleanor, the retired botanical illustrator who owns the cottage, has already left out the nightly offering: a hand-painted acorn cap, its curved surface dotted with tiny purple violets, full of honeyed lavender syrup, and a crumb of shortbread flecked with lemon zest beside it. Lira’s absolute favorite part of this ritual comes first: she creeps forward, nose twitching at the sweet, floral scent, and presses her cheek to the smooth, paint-sealed edge of the acorn cap before she even dares to taste the syrup. She can feel the faint, raised lines of the brushstrokes Eleanor used to paint the violets, the warm, sun-baked wood of the cap that’s held Eleanor’s gentle hands all day, and for a quiet second, it feels like a tiny, secret hello between them, no words needed.
She dips her tiny pink nose into the syrup next, lapping slow, the lavender sweet and light, the honey thick and warm on her tongue, before gnawing at the crumbly shortbread. She’s just about to turn back to the baseboard gap, syrup sticky on her whiskers, when her tail flicks, and the soft tip catches a loose loop of cream cotton thread dangling from Eleanor’s open sewing basket, left there when she’d dashed to answer the door an hour prior. Lira freezes, heart thudding so hard she can feel it in her ears. The loop is tangled tight around the tip of her tail, and if she tugs to get free, the thread will yank the tiny thimble-sized jar of dried chamomile sitting beside the acorn cap, sending it clattering across the tile straight toward Mimsy. She holds perfectly still, wiggling her tail back and forth so slow the thread loop slips free inch by inch, not daring to breathe as Mimsy lets out a soft, sleepy mew in her sleep. When she’s free, she darts back to the baseboard gap so fast her paws skid, leaving a tiny smudge of syrup on the counter where her paw had rested.
The next morning, Eleanor comes into the kitchen, spots the pulled cotton loop, the tiny syrup smudge, and smiles, because she knows exactly who left them. That night, when Lira peeks out of the baseboard gap, the acorn cap is new, painted with tiny white clover, and the syrup inside is mixed with dandelion honey, her favorite. Tucked under the edge of the cap is a tiny scrap of soft, unbleached linen, frayed at the ends so there are no sharp loops to catch her tail, and a single pressed dandelion petal, sealed with a dot of beeswax, a drop of honey glistening on its yellow surface.
Lira’s whiskers twitch, and she’s suddenly three years younger, scrawny and small, her fur bright orange from brushing against marigolds in the cottage garden the week she’d moved into the baseboard gap. She’d been too shy to approach the acorn cap Eleanor had left out that first week, too scared of the big, soft human who sat at the kitchen table sketching flowers all day. She’d stood on her hind legs on the counter edge, nose twitching at the sweet smell of the syrup, when Eleanor looked up from her sketchbook. She didn’t move, didn’t make a sound, just went outside, picked a dandelion from the garden, put a single drop of honey on its petal, and left it on the counter edge, low enough for Lira to reach. Lira had eaten the honey, sweet and tangy, and looked up to see Eleanor smiling, not moving to shoo her away. That was the first time she knew she wasn’t just a pest in the walls—she was seen.
She presses her cheek to the new clover-painted acorn cap, then dips her nose into the dandelion-lavender syrup, the taste sweeter than any she’s had before. Somewhere above her, Mimsy purrs in her sleep, and the kitchen smells like honey and lavender and old pine, and for Lira, it’s the best, coziest night she’s ever had.

### Bramble’s Human Snack Bakery (No Stakes, No Morals, Very Sticky Paws)
The root system of the 300-year-old oak tree is cool and mossy even at noon, when sun dapples through the leaves to gild the edges of Bramble’s bakery counter: a flat, golden chanterelle mushroom cap propped between two gnarled oak roots. Bramble, a thumb-sized hedgehog with a snuffly pink nose and one slightly bent spine quill, stands on a dented apple juice crate to reach the top shelf of his hollowed-out mushroom stem storage unit. His apron is a scrap of faded kombucha bottle label, printed with wobbly letters that read *GUT LICE* (the G faded off weeks ago, and Bramble has not noticed), tied around his middle with a strand of dandelion fluff.

His cash register is an acorn cap glued to the counter, brimming with smooth grey pebbles, dried blackberry seeds, a lost button from a cardinal’s sweater, and a tiny iridescent plastic bead a hiker dropped last Tuesday. Bramble’s entire menu is scrawled on birch bark with crushed blackberry juice, and every single item is a wildly incorrect interpretation of human snacks he pieced together from a pair of teen hikers who sat on the oak roots two weekends prior, munching loudly enough for him to hear as he hid under a maple leaf scribbling notes with a pine needle.

Today’s specials are:
1. POP-TARTS (HUMAN FAVORITE)
2. CHEESE PUFFS
3. FROSTED DONUTS

The first customer of the day is Thistle, a young rabbit with floppy ears still matted from morning foraging. She hops in, nose twitching, and leans on the counter with a paw. “Good morning, Bramble! I found a whole patch of clover this morning but it’s boring. Do you have anything sweet?”
Bramble puffs out his chest, proud, and lifts the cover off a tiny clay dish to reveal his pop-tarts: two thin rounds of pine needle shortbread pressed together with honey-infused pine resin, wrapped in tiny scraps of the blue wrapper the hiker dropped, printed with little pine trees. He hands one to Thistle.
She takes a bite. Her ears shoot straight up. She chews slowly, blinking a few times, then smiles. “Oh! Oh my. This is very piney. And sweet. Like if a Christmas tree had a honey birthday. It’s very nice, thank you. I’ll take two, please.” She pays with two shiny blackberry seeds, which Bramble tucks into his acorn cap, wiping his sticky honey paws on his apron and leaving a little smudge on the *GUT LICE* print that makes the L look like a T.

Next in is Mr. Brock, an elderly badger who wears a tweed cap and a miniature flannel cravat. He trundles in leaning on a twig walking stick, and nods formally at Bramble. “Good afternoon, young hedgehog. I’m in need of a light snack to accompany my dandelion root tea. What do you recommend?”
Bramble gestures proudly to a bowl of his cheese puffs: puffy balls of dandelion fluff (the lightest thing he could find, since the hiker said “cheese puffs float”) dusted with nutritional yeast he found in a crumpled hiker bag, which he assumed was “human yellow magic snack dust.” He scoops a tiny pawful into a moss-lined dish and slides it across the counter.
Mr. Brock takes one. It’s so light it poofs against his tongue, dusting his white whiskers with yellow flakes. He blinks, adjusts his cravat, and nods. “Mmm. Remarkably airy. It has a nutty, almost umami quality? Though I confess I cannot locate any actual cheese. It’s a most unique confection, my dear. Do you have any moss napkins? I’ve got yellow all over my cravat, and my wife will have my head if I get it dirty before the forest potluck.”
Bramble hands him a moss napkin, and Mr. Brock pays with a smooth white river pebble.

The last customer of the morning is Nutmeg, a hyper young squirrel who zips in so fast she skids on the root entrance and almost knocks over the birch bark menu. “BRAMBLE! I need a treat! I stole an acorn from Old Man Squirrel’s stash and I feel so guilty I could chew through a tree trunk!”
Bramble grins and lifts the cover off another dish to reveal his frosted donuts: whole white clover flower heads glazed with rosewater syrup he made from foraged rose petals, dotted with tiny bits of purple clover petal as “sprinkles” (the hiker said donuts had rainbow sprinkles, and clover was the closest colorful round thing Bramble could find). He hands one to Nutmeg.
She takes a huge bite, stops chittering mid-sentence, and blinks so hard her whole body wiggles. “Whoa. This tastes like if a rose bush had a sugar high. It’s so good! But… it’s crunchy? And it has flowers in it? Donuts are supposed to be squishy, right? The hikers said donuts are squishy.”
Bramble shrugs, his spines rustling. “The humans ate them this way. I saw.”
Nutmeg shrugs too, and eats the rest of the donut in one bite, paying with a dried sunflower seed she had tucked in her cheek.

---
By midafternoon, the customers have trickled off. A beetle paid a shiny green beetle shell for a crumb of pine shortbread, which Bramble tucked into his acorn cap next to the button. He sits now on his apple juice crate, counting his earnings: four blackberry seeds, one white pebble, one sunflower seed, one button, one plastic bead, one beetle shell. He eats the leftover clover donut himself; it’s a little stale, but the rosewater syrup is still sticky and sweet.
He yawns, pulling a tiny flannel blanket (cut from the same scrap as Mr. Brock’s cravat, found by the roots last week) over his legs. Tomorrow, he’s going to try making “BBQ chips” from a crumpled bag of human chips he found: he’ll slice mushroom caps thin, dust them with crushed blackberry, and add a little bit of dirt, because the bag looked brown and he assumed that was the “BBQ” part. The oak tree creaks above him, fireflies flicker through the gaps in the roots, and Bramble falls asleep behind the counter, the smell of pine and honey and rosewater clinging to his spines.
No one learned anything. No one’s life changed. It was a very good day.

dream_response:
okay so first off the swings, right? if you pump hard enough to get over the top bar you get a free punch in the arm from whoever’s standing closest but also if you don’t scream while you touch the cloud that’s low that day you get a wish which everyone believed even though no one ever actually touched a cloud and also if you brought a bag of orange Cheetos you could barter for a swing turn if someone claimed it when the bell rang, white Cheetos were for trades only not actual payment that was the unwritten rule. Cool Ranch Doritos were the top currency, then original Cheetos, then Fruit Roll-Ups, and if you could pull a Fruit by the Foot all the way across the lunch table without breaking it that was almost as good as Cool Ranch, you were playground royalty for ten minutes.

then the made up games, ghost in the graveyard but not the spooky one, you stand in a circle and someone’s it and they have to freeze like a ghost when someone yells “ghost in the graveyard!” but if you move while you yell it you’re grounded for three turns, everyone cheated at that all the time. wall ball but you could only use the red rubber playground balls, blue ones were for kickball only, if you used a blue one the 5th grade hall monitors in those orange sashes would take it for a week, but if you gave them blue sour candy straws they’d let you off, green straws were gross no one wanted those. 5th graders were basically tyrants but if you brought them a pack of Fruit Gushers they’d let you run in the hall, which was the biggest flex ever.

snack rituals: Pokémon card trade rules that no one wrote down, first edition holographic Charizard was worth three pepperoni Lunchables, but only if the hologram had no scratches, a scratched one was only worth one Lunchable and a pack of Fruit Gushers. pizza Lunchables made you basically rich, ham Lunchables were the worst, you could only trade those for a cool pencil or a sticker. the secret snack stash under the big slide, if you left an unopened snack there after recess and it was still there the next day it was safe, if someone took it you had to leave a note saying “I know you took my Cheetos” and then you could take one of theirs the next day, but only if you left a “fair trade” note back otherwise it was full playground war. there was that one kid who took everyone’s snacks once and someone put a whole pickle in his lunchbox and he cried, pickles were currency for like two weeks after that.

the dumb urban legends everyone 100% believed: step on a crack break your mother’s back unless you knock on wood immediately after, everyone kept a tiny piece of wood in their pocket to knock on if they stepped on a crack. purple M&Ms meant you had a crush on someone, but only if you ate it without looking at the color first, if you looked it reversed the crush so everyone dumped all their M&Ms out and picked a random one to eat to either confirm the crush or make the person they liked have a crush on them. cootie shots—you draw a little dot on your wrist with a pen and say the rhyme and now the person can’t get cooties, if someone had cooties all you had to do was give them a cootie shot and they were fine. the rubber and glue thing, say “I’m rubber you’re glue” three times fast while looking at the bully and whatever mean thing they said bounces off you and sticks to them, never actually worked but everyone swears it did. the legend that there was a secret tunnel under the monkey bars leading to an abandoned house down the street full of old baseball cards and infinite candy, no one ever looked for it because the old man who lived there had a BB gun and would shoot you. also the one where if you said “bloody Mary” three times in the bathroom at recess she’d give you candy if you didn’t scream, everyone tried it once but no one ever actually did it because they were scared she’d be mad.

tiny secret joys: the metal slide getting so hot in the summer it almost burns your butt but you don’t care, you run your hand along it before you slide down. spinning on the merry-go-round so fast you almost throw up and then the whole world spins and you laugh so hard you can’t stand up. if you can climb all the way to the top of the jungle gym without touching the ground you’re king/queen of the whole recess, everyone has to obey your dumb rules like “hop on one foot for 10 seconds” or “give me a Fruit Roll-Up”. secret handshakes with your best friend that were 10 complicated steps, if a stranger tried to learn it you’d say “it’s a secret” and walk away. chalk drawings on the blacktop, draw your name in bubble letters with stars and hearts, if someone writes over your name it’s a huge fight, if you write “best friends” with someone’s name you’re friends forever even if you fight the next day.

snack trade hierarchy that no one wrote down: Dunkaroos were top tier, you could get a whole Lunchable and a pack of Pokémon cards for one pack. then gummy fruit snacks, especially dinosaur or Pokémon shaped ones. then chewy fruit snacks, those were worth less. Cool Ranch Doritos were worth two Lunchables, peach snacks were worth half a Lunchable, cheese puffs in the cylinder you shared with everyone but you got the first handful as a tax. Lunchable prizes: temporary tattoos and stickers were worth two Fruit Roll-Ups, the dumb plastic pencil sharpeners were worth one sticker max, no one wanted them. erasers: scented strawberry ones were worth a whole Lunchable, pink non-smudge ones were two Fruit Roll-Ups, white ones were one, bent holographic Pokémon cards were worth three Lunchables but only if you said “it’s holographic, it’s okay to be bent” when you traded it otherwise they could say it was damaged and take it back. you couldn’t trade a card you opened from a pack for at least 24 hours or it was “new card luck” and you’d get ripped off, that was the golden rule.

playground pranks and unwritten game rules: four square, if the ball hits the line it’s a do-over, no arguments, if it hits the ceiling it’s out. if you hit the ball so hard it goes over the fence you’re king of four square for the whole recess, if it goes into the street the hall monitor takes the ball for the whole rest of the day and everyone hates you. tag: if you say “time out” you’re safe for 10 seconds, “time in” to get back in, if you tag someone on the swings or slide they have to come down before they’re it, that’s not up for debate. base is always the metal pole by the wall or the slide, if you touch it you’re safe. seesaw: you can’t get off until the other person says it’s okay, if you drop them you have to bring them a snack the next day as apology. sandbox: you can’t dig a hole next to someone else’s without asking, if you do you have to share the sand you dig out. if you make a good sandcastle the 5th graders will kick it over unless you have a big friend group to protect it. hopscotch: if you land on the same square twice you have to do a spin before you throw your rock, if you miss the square you’re out, if you throw the rock on the line you get an extra turn, if you close your eyes when you land on home you get a whole extra turn. jump rope rhymes that no one knew the meaning of: Cinderella dressed in yella went upstairs to kiss her fella missed a step and broke her leg now she’s in the hospital with a big egg, Teddy Bear Teddy Bear turn around touch the ground, if you trip the rope you have to say “red rubber” and you’re out. double dutch was the ultimate flex, if you could do it without tripping you got to pick the rhythm and lead the game.

other random stuff everyone believed: if you dig a hole in the sandbox deep enough you’ll get to China, recess monitors always made you fill it in so no one got deeper than a foot. dandelion seeds: blow them off and make a wish, it only comes true if you don’t tell anyone what you wished for. if you sit under the big oak tree at exactly 3pm you’ll see the playground squirrel that wears a tiny hat, if you give him a peanut he’ll leave you a shiny rock, 5th graders told the little kids that one to mess with them. the legend that if you leave your backpack under the bench overnight the playground fairies fill it with candy, one kid said he did it and got a bag of Skittles so everyone believed it for a month. the one about the old abandoned house: if you knock on the door three times and run, a ghost will leave a candy bar on the porch, no one ever actually did it because the old man had a BB gun.

snack hacks everyone knew: if you mix chocolate milk and orange milk it tastes like a chocolate orange, most of the time it’s gross but if you get the ratio right it’s the best thing ever. mix Sprite and Fanta it tastes like cream soda. if you have bubble gum in your hair peanut butter gets it out, so a jar of peanut butter was worth a whole pack of Pokémon cards for gum emergencies. if you save your milk carton and turn it into a little container you can carry extra snacks to recess, as long as you wash it out first. the drinking fountain by the gym tasted way better than the one by the classrooms, no one knew why but there was always a line for it.

other tiny joys: finding a four leaf clover by the fence, you keep it in your notebook for luck. finding a lost eraser that’s shaped like a pizza or has a scratch and sniff smell, everyone’s jealous. if you have a pair of clear gogo boots with lights on the soles you’re the coolest kid ever, everyone follows you around under the slide to see the lights. if you have a Game Boy Color everyone crowds around you at lunch to watch you play Pokémon, you let them play for a snack each. if you can blow a bubble bigger than your face everyone claps, you’re famous for the day. if you spin on the merry-go-round so fast your legs fly off the ground you feel like you can fly. getting a gold star on your paper, you show it off to everyone at recess like it’s a trophy. being line leader when you walk back to class, you get to hold the door and pick the song everyone walks to. finding a holographic Pokémon card on the blacktop, you yell “found a card!” and everyone runs over, if it’s good you’re famous for the week.

all the fake little rules everyone just went along with: if you say the alphabet backwards in front of the slide a monster comes out, 5th graders told the 1st graders that. if you eat a leaf from the bush by the bathroom you grow a tail, same thing. if you leave your coat on the bench overnight it gets stolen by the playground ghosts, no one ever left their coat out overnight after that one kid said his did. if you step on a fire ant hill you have to take your shoe off and shake it out, even if they’re just regular ants everyone was scared of them. scratch and sniff stickers: strawberry and blueberry are worth three regular stickers, pizza scented is worth a whole Lunchable. if you lend someone a pencil and they break it they owe you two new ones, that’s non-negotiable. if you lose someone’s pencil you have to buy them a new one even if it’s a 10 cent one, that’s playground law. if you share a snack with someone they have to share back later, if they don’t they’re a mooch and no one trades with them. if you have a scrunchie with a neon flower on it it’s worth two Fruit Roll-Ups. if you have a spork shaped like a dinosaur you can trade it for a whole pack of Fruit Gushers. if you sit on the top of the slide at exactly 12pm you’ll see the school ghost wave at you, everyone said they saw it but no one ever actually sat there because they were scared. if you put a penny on the railroad tracks near the school it’ll flatten into a cool shape worth a whole pack of Pokémon cards, no one ever did it because the crossing guard yelled but everyone talked about it. if you leave a candy in the sandbox overnight the sand fairies will leave you a cooler candy, no one ever checked but everyone left candy there after recess sometimes.

and the inside recess stuff we all hated: you have to sit at your desk and read or play quiet games, if you’re really quiet the teacher lets you play hangman, winner gets a sticker. if you forgot your umbrella and it’s raining you have to run to the car line and if you get soaked everyone laughs, unless you have a raincoat with ears on the hood then everyone’s jealous. if you’re the teacher’s helper you get to pass out papers and sneak a look at the candy jar on her desk, that’s the only good part of inside recess. if you get to play Oregon Trail on the classroom computer, if you die of dysentery everyone teases you for weeks, if you make it to Oregon you’re a legend.

all of it was totally undocumented, no one wrote any of these rules down, we all just knew them, and half of them were made up by 5th graders to mess with the little kids, and none of it mattered at all but it was the most important stuff in the whole world for 20 minutes every day at recess.
